description: 1 - 19-12-2024 - Google Chat Messenger exchange between Vincent Devine (Government Chief Security Officer, CO) and Junior Civil Servant (JCS

participants.name: Google Chat Messenger exchange between Vincent Devine

text:
Chief Security Officer, CO) and Junior Civil Servant (JCS

[19/12/2024, 08:27] JCS : Morning Vincent, on the vetting issue yday from
Gerard, a brief update from MR

| spoke to Gerard - it appears that there is a chance / it is likely that the individual in question
is in the HoL. On that basis, | pointed Gerard towards the GSB paper from October / Tom B
as such members don’t need to go through NSV

So, we may not need to process. Will get details today and of course we stand ready to
prioritise etc...

[19/12/2024, 08:31] Vincent Devine: Thanks. Can you chase the team on the vetting/STRAP
letter?

Sorry. That is one from yesterday that | didn’t hit send on.

[19/12/2024, 08:31] JCS no probs - Vita and JCS have been in there, there are
a few comments

[19/12/2024, 08:36] Vincent Devine: That is good on the FCDO issue. Can you dig out the
paper to see if there is flexibility? Eg, the Foreign Sec might wish to have a process in place
in any case to offer reassurance. | am also conscious the individual will need STRAP. Does
the paper say anything about DV remaining a pre-condition for that?

[19/12/2024, 08:37] JCS : yep we'll have a look now

[19/12/2024, 08:38] JCS : Looking at the paper DV for these individuals is not a
pre-condition for STRAP. The paper says

Whilst these individuals do not require NSV, if they require access to information or assets

classified at "STRAP", bailed _ Incertain
circumstances a STRAP application can be requested from those listed on the exemption
list, if it is believed to be necessary by ile . All STRAP inductees

are subject to aiid

Please contact your Security Team or local STRAP
Security Officer (STRAPSO) for further information.

[19/12/2024, 08:39] JCS : For public appointments, On a case-by-case basis - the
minister responsible for the public appointment role may request individuals listed
(paragraph 1) to undergo NSV. The Permanent Secretary has delegated authority to make
this decision as well.

What | not sure about is whether the role is a public appointment, | don’t think it is really. A
public appointment is for eg like the Childrens Commissioner

Google doc link

[19/12/2024, 08:42] Vincent Devine: Thanks. Grateful if Michael could draw the STRAP.
requirements to Gerard's attention. It will be worth asking the FCDO STRAPSO to rapidly
confirm with the authorities whether (or not) they need a STRAP application. They might...

[19/12/2024, 09:42] JCS ok

formally announced. | would be grateful if you can start the vetting process - | believe that as

Rochelle

From: lan Collard *

To: lain Griffiths

ce: JCS ; Philip Barton (PUS) ; Rochelle Fisher ; Gerard McGurk ; JCS ; Corin

Robertson ; PUS Action ; Mervyn Thomas ; Robert Tinline ; Harriet Mathews

Subject: Personal/Lord Mandelson security clearance

Dear lain,
We have spoken twice today. For wider awareness:

* Cabinet Office Government Security Group Director, Tom Bramley, called me this
morning about the security clearance requirements for Lord Mandelson, HMA-designate
Washington.
+ Although Gerard McGurk had been in touch with GSG late last week, and GSG had
stated that the ‘fit and proper person’ clearance exemption for Lords and MPs should
apply, CO had reflected over the weekend, and judged that the exemptions policy had
not envisaged the unusual situation of MPs/Lords being appointed to Ambassadorial
positions.
+ ESND have checked the precedent, and can confirm that clearance was previously
required of an MP appointed to an HMA position.
+ Additionally, we all recognise that the US are strict about clearance matters, and will
likely check Lord Mandelson’s clearance with ESND ahead of any sensitive discussions
he will be invited to upon arrival in Washington. Failure to report the correct clearance
could have an awkward impact, which we would want to avoid.

34

• The upshot is that ESND and Government Security Group agree that Lord
Mandelson should undergo the normal DV clearance procedure.
• I have discussed with the PUS, who is content that we proceed accordingly.
• ESND (
, in copy) has set up a profile for Lord
Mandelson on the vetting system, and has emailed him today with details on how to
complete the procedure.
• You will kindly flag it to him when you speak at 3pm today.
• Cabinet Office are ready to expedite the clearance. The quicker Lord Mandelson
proceeds with the paperwork, the quicker we can get the clearance processed.
To note, while Lord Mandelson is in pre-deployment preparations in KCS during January, 
ESND are content to afford him the ‘fit and proper’ exemption to access the building 
unaccompanied, and to access Osprey IT and material up to Secret. I am sure the PUS will 
want to judge access to any material above Secret ahead of a formal DV clearance coming 
through.
For the purposes of the role in Washington, Lord Mandelson will have to apply for STRAP 
clearance in the usual way (once DV is secured). Again, ESND can seek an expedited 
decision.
Best wishes,
Ian

Dear Corin, JCS (for Olly),

An update. This information is provided in strictest confidence, to respect vetting confidence
principles, because Corin and Olly are directly in my line management chain as Chief Security
Officer.

ESND have approved the DV clearance of Lord Mandelson. He was sent notification and his
DV certificate by email (on Osprey) on the evening of 29 January 2025. It will be valid for
five years.

As part of the usual clearance process, UKSV identified some areas in his application for
ESND to review:

* Tunderstand that Lord Mandelson’s private sector engagements are being managed by
HRD and Legal Directorate through the conflict of interest process.

¢ With regard to personal conduct, I understand that Lord Mandelson has received a
letter from Mervyn Thomas, informing him of his responsibilities as an FCDO
employee, including under the Diplomatic Service Regulations.

72

* Matters pertaining to his overseas contacts will certainly be reviewed by the STRAP
Authorities. *** , 1am unable to assure about the
decision they will take. Their decision will determine the access that Lord Mandelson
has to the most sensitive information and meetings.

ESND shared with Lord Mandelson (and separately with JCS in Americas
Directorate) the STRAP application forms, which we have asked Lord Mandelson to complete
as an urgent priority. We will seek to expedite the processing of his application as soon as the
completed forms are retumed to us.

Best wishes,

Tan

OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE 
--- Resending to correct email ----  
Elkie,  
Grateful if you can commission CO/UKSV to input into an Annex that will be shared 
with the FAC in response to their attached letter re. Mandelson. Grateful if we can have 
input by 2pm today please as we are aiming for FS to issue the letter today. Given 
FCDO do not own the security clearance process, the Foreign Secretary will not want 
to take responsibility for responding to the FAC on these matters. We therefore 
propose the FS write a short cover letter and an annex (owned by the Cabinet Office) 
with responses to their questions.  
To support your teams, the questions are set out below and I have included where we 
think the FCDO does have agency to answer the questions are they refer specifically 
to FCDO/the FS.  
Annex A - Cabinet Office response to the FAC questions  
1. What security concerns were raised by the agencies undertaking security
clearance ahead of Lord Mandelson’s appointment? [For CO to answer]
2. What was the response of the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development
Office to any such concerns? FCDO advice was not sought, nor given.
3. Was it the assessment of the FCDO that any such concerns represented a
potential barrier to Lord Mandelson’s appointment? FCDO advice was not
sought, nor given.
4. Were any conditions imposed on the remit of Lord Mandelson as Ambassador
in order to mitigate any security concerns? [For CO to answer]
5. Was there a decision made to dismiss security concerns? Was such a
decision taken by the FCDO or by No. 10? If so, by whom? [For CO to answer]
6. If no decision was taken to straightforwardly dismiss security concerns raised
by the relevant agencies, was a decision made to suspend or alter: (a) the
usual security vetting requirements; or, (b) the usual timeline for vetting
procedures? [For CO to answer]
7. Was it the assessment of the Foreign Secretary that there was sufficient time
between the announcement of Lord Mandelson’s appointment in December
and the beginning of his tenure on 10 February to carry out full thorough
vetting? Yes. While an expedited clearance process is faster than a routine
clearance, it is not unusual to do so, and within the bounds of the vetting
process.
8. Did the usual vetting process for diplomatic appointments commence in
relation to Lord Mandelson before his appointment was made public? [For CO
to answer]
Kind regards,  
 
 | Deputy Private Secretary to the Permanent Under 
Secretaries, Sir Oliver Robbins and Nick Dyer | Permanent Under-Secretaries’ Joint 
Private Office  
 | King Charles Street | Foreign, Commonwealth & Development

description: 32 - 15-09-2025 - Email between lan Collard (former Chief Property & Security Officer, FCDO) and others
text:
Officer, FCDO) and others

From: lan Collard * Personal

Sent: Mon 15/09/2025 9:55:39 AM (UTC)

To: Mark Power Personal Catherine-Naomi Jobshare
Personal

Ce: Jcs Personal

Subject: Clearance issue

Mark, Catherine,
An outstanding issue is whether ESND should lapse, suspend or withdraw
Mandelson’s DV clearance? While practically it will make little difference, because
Post have already removed his access to the embassy, taken his laptop, suspended
his FCDO email account and stopped sharing any official information, there is a
presentational aspect.
| can imagine, for example, a PQ that asks — presume you have taken his clearance
away from him now? (And suggestions of incompetence if we haven't.)
But | presume there are also employment law issues still at stake? Hence, | don’t
want ESND to act precipitously. Grateful for views. Including ultimately from Olly,
once we have considered advice for him.
Distinction (as | understand it) between different ESND options:
+ Lapse — someone no longer works for FCDO; we put the clearance into
abeyance (including on the official vetting system) and wouldn't confirm it is
extant anymore
+ Suspend — we have concerns about the clearance being active, and inform the
individual we are taking the clearance from them (with commensurate
implications) while concerns are reviewed
+ Withdraw — we completely remove the clearance, again ordinarily informing the

individual
Views welcome.
Thanks,
lan
lan Collard | Chief Property & Security Officer and Director Estates, Security & Network
Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office | Personal

Chief of Staff. Personal | Executive Officer Personal

OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE  
--- Resending to correct email ----  
Elkie,  
Grateful if you can commission CO/UKSV to input into an Annex that will be shared with the 
FAC in response to their attached letter re. Mandelson. Grateful if we can have input by 2pm 
today please as we are aiming for FS to issue the letter today.  
Given FCDO do not own the security clearance process, the Foreign Secretary will not want 
to take responsibility for responding to the FAC on these matters. We therefore propose the 
FS write a short cover letter and an annex (owned by the Cabinet Office) with responses to 
their questions.  
To support your teams, the questions are set out below and I have included where we think 
the FCDO does have agency to answer the questions are they refer specifically to FCDO/the 
FS.  
Annex A - Cabinet Office response to the FAC questions 
1. What security concerns were raised by the agencies undertaking security clearance ahead
of Lord Mandelson’s appointment? [For CO to answer]
2. What was the response of the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office to any
such concerns? FCDO advice was not sought, nor given.
3. Was it the assessment of the FCDO that any such concerns represented a potential barrier
to Lord Mandelson’s appointment? FCDO advice was
not sought, nor given.  
4. Were any conditions imposed on the remit of Lord Mandelson as Ambassador in
order to mitigate any security concerns? [For CO to answer]
5. Was there a decision made to dismiss security concerns? Was such a
decision taken by the FCDO or by No. 10? If so, by whom? [For CO to answer]
6. If no decision was taken to straightforwardly dismiss security concerns raised by the
relevant agencies, was a decision made to suspend or alter: (a) the usual security
vetting requirements; or, (b) the usual timeline for vetting procedures? [For CO to
answer]
7. Was it the assessment of the Foreign Secretary that there was sufficient time
between the announcement of Lord Mandelson’s appointment in December and the
beginning of his tenure on 10 February to carry out full thorough vetting? Yes. While an
expedited clearance process is faster than a routine clearance, it is not unusual to do
so, and within the bounds of the vetting process.
8. Did the usual vetting process for diplomatic appointments commence in
relation to Lord Mandelson before his appointment was made public? [For
CO to answer]

[TOP]

Dear Prime Minister,

In discussion with Foreign Office, Cabinet Office and No10 officials, I have looked into whether the
correct process was followed for both the appointment of HMA Washington, Peter Mandelson, and the
process around your decision to withdraw him from post. As you know this was a political diplomatic
appointment, and therefore - as set out in the note from my predecessor, which you received before
making this appointment - requires the necessary security clearances and doing due diligence on any
potential Conflicts of Interest or other issues of which you should be aware.

In both the appointment and the withdrawal, I have seen no evidence that proper processes were not
carried out adequately. In my discussions with officials I have reviewed or discussed:

- the Propriety and Ethics open source due diligence that you saw as part of the appointment process:

- the post appointment vetting which was completed post announcement but before he took up post; and
- your decision to withdraw HMA Washington once the content of the emails was known and HMA
Washington was given the right to reply to his line manager, Oliver Robbins (FCDO Permanent Under
Secretary)

I will keep this under review if any further evidence emerges.

Thanks Olly, I've put the dates in below, and added the penultimate paragraph as 
Chris was keen to give the PM assurances on the processes being carried out properly 
as far as he has seen. Let me know if you have strong views on that.

Prime Minister  
In discussion with Foreign Office, Cabinet Office and No10 officials, I have looked into whether the 
correct process was followed for both the appointment of the previous HMA Washington, Peter 
Mandelson, and the process around your decision to withdraw him from post.  
The process for his appointment was unusual but not irregular. This was a direct Prime Ministerial 
appointment from outside either the Diplomatic or Home Civil Service. You received advice from my 
predecessor on 11 November 2024 before deciding on this course. That advice explained your right to 
Personal

115

make such an appointment and the process for doing so but did not give specific advice on candidates. 
Following that advice, the Propriety and Ethics Team (PET) conducted an open-source due diligence 
report on Lord Mandelson. This identified his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein as a reputational risk. 
Your special advisers then obtained further assurances from Lord Mandelson on this specific issue 
which I have not been sighted on. No evidence has come to light suggesting concerns were raised with 
you or my predecessor on national security grounds as part of the pre-appointment timeline. On 18 
December 2024 your office wrote to the previous Permanent Under-Secretary at the FCDO instructing 
him to seek The King's approval for the appointment, which was duly sought later the same day. The 
appointment was announced on 20 December 2024. The FCDO immediately commenced the national 
security vetting process, submitting Lord Mandelson as a priority candidate for Developed Vetting to 
the UK Security Vetting Agency. Clearance was duly given before he commenced his posting on 10 
February 2025. The FCDO also took on the task of working with Lord Mandelson to understand and 
mitigate conflicts of interest in advance of his departure for Washington. [This process was also 
concluded before his departure for Washington.]  
Your decision to withdraw Lord Mandelson as HMA Washington was taken in the light of your belief 
that his account of his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein at the time of appointment had been less than 
totally candid, and that his replies on questions posed by his line manager, the FCDO Permanent 
Under-Secretary, left open the possibility that further damaging revelations would ensue. The FCDO 
is now in correspondence with Lord Mandelson about the termination of his contract. [In both the 
appointment and the decision to withdraw I have seen no evidence that proper processes were not 
carried out adequately.]  
I will keep this under review if any further evidence emerges.  
Elkie Symes  
Principal Private Secretary to the Cabinet Secretary

70 Whitehall, London, SW1A 2AS

OFFICIAL – SENSITIVE - PERSONAL 
OFFICIAL – SENSITIVE PERSONAL 
From the Cabinet Secretary 
16 September 2025 
PRIME MINISTER 
APPOINTMENT AND WITHDRAWAL FROM POST OF HMA 
WASHINGTON 
As we discussed, I have looked into whether the correct process was followed for both 
the appointment of the previous HMA Washington, Peter Mandelson, and the process 
around your decision to withdraw him from post. 
The process for his appointment was unusual but not irregular. This was a direct 
Prime Ministerial appointment from outside both the Diplomatic and Home Civil 
Service. You received advice from my predecessor on 11 November 2024 before 
deciding on this type of appointment. That advice explained your right to make such 
an appointment and the process for doing so but did not give specific advice on 
candidates.  The process that took place followed the advice my predecessor gave in 
particular “for them to acquire the necessary security clearances and do due diligence 
on any potential conflicts of interest or other issues of which you should be aware 
before confirming your choice.” All processes set out by my predecessor took place. 
Two actions, the security vetting and the process on conflicts of interest took place 
after the decision to appoint. I do not however consider that to be material as the 
vetting process was complete before the previous HMA Washington took up post on 
10 February 2025, and it is more usual for security vetting to happen after 
appointment. 
Following the advice from my predecessor, the Propriety and Ethics Team (PET) 
conducted an open-source due diligence report on Lord Mandelson. This identified his 
relationship with Jeffrey Epstein as a reputational risk.  Your special advisers then 
obtained further assurances from Lord Mandelson on this specific issue. No evidence 
has come to light suggesting concerns were raised with you or my predecessor on 
national security grounds as part of the pre-appointment timeline.  On 18 December 
2024 your office wrote to the previous Permanent Under-Secretary at the FCDO

description: 37 - 16-09-2025 - Letter from Sir Chris Wormald (former Cabinet Secretary) 119
text:
119

OFFICIAL – SENSITIVE - PERSONAL 
OFFICIAL – SENSITIVE PERSONAL 
instructing him to seek The King's approval for the appointment, which was duly 
sought later the same day. 
The appointment was announced on 20 December 2024.  The FCDO immediately 
commenced the national security vetting process, submitting Lord Mandelson as a 
priority candidate for Developed Vetting to the UK Security Vetting Agency.  
Clearance was duly given before he commenced his posting on 10 February 2025.  
The FCDO also immediately took on the task of working with Lord Mandelson to 
understand and mitigate conflicts of interest in advance of his departure for 
Washington. 
Taken together this has met all of the actions set out in my predecessor’s advice of 11 
November. 
Your decision to withdraw Lord Mandelson as HMA Washington was taken in light 
of your belief that his account of his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein at the time of 
appointment had been less than totally candid, and that his replies on questions posed 
by his line manager, the FCDO Permanent Under-Secretary, left open the possibility 
that further damaging revelations would ensue. The FCDO is now in correspondence 
with Lord Mandelson about the termination of his contract. 
The evidence I have reviewed leads me to conclude that appropriate processes were 
followed in both the appointment and withdrawal of the former HMA Washington. 
I will keep this under review if any further evidence emerges. 
SIR CHRIS WORMALD KCB 
Cabinet Secretary and Head of the Civil Service

Hiall - here's current draft of the Q&A.

@Rochelle Fisher would you mind sending me the relevant section of the CRGA? Thank you so much,

O&A
‘When does he start?
He will take up his post in January 2025.
Will he be in post ahead of inauguration?
That is the expectation, but we will set this out in due course.
‘Whats his status - will he be a Spad?
No ~ the role remains a civil service post.
‘Who made the appointment?
This was a political appointment, which is provided for under the Constitutional Reform and Governance
Act 2010.
Tt was approved by the Prime Minister and the Foreign Secretary.
So why not appoint a civil servant? Is there previous precedent for political appointments to HMA roles?
It is not unusual to make political appointments in the diplomatic service. This is provided for under the
Constitutional Reform and Govemance Act 2010.
Previous political appointees as Heads of Mission in recent years include:
* Alastair Goodlad (British High Commissioner to Australia 2000-2005)
* Paul Boateng (British High Commissioner to South Africa 2005 — 2009)
* Helen Liddell (British High Commissioner to Australia 2005 — 2009)
* Baroness Amos (British High Commissioner to Australia 2009 — 2010)
* Ed Llewellyn (Ambassador to France 2016-2022 and then Ambassador to Italy)
* George Hollingberry (current HMA to Cuba)

First Jonathan Powell, now Peter Mandelson - just appointing friends to top jobs?

All appointments are made on the basis of having the right skills and experience for the role. Lord
Mandelson is a highly respected and experienced figure who will bring the required skills to the role as we
build relationships with the new administration and continue to strengthen our partnership.

Tt is not unusual to make political appointments in the diplomatic service and this is permitted under the
Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010.

When does Karen leaye?

As we have previously set out, she will leave her post in January 2025. [Can go further here if it’s
confirmed?]

130


Why are you letting her go when she holds all the relationships with the Trump team?

Karen has completed her four years in the role, which is the usual duration for an Ambassador posting, and
we have always said she will move on in January 2025 - this follows an extension to her original contract
in order to lead the Embassy through the recent U.S. election.

The Prime Minster has thanked her for her outstanding service in her role.

Has Trump agreed?

The usual appointments process has been followed.

We have informed both the current U.S. administration and the incoming administration. [any further we can
go here would be helpful - think this is being handled on the No 10 side though]

Has Foreign Secretary been informed?

Yes, he approved the appointment.

Palace informed?

Yes, in the usual way and the King approved the appointment on the recommendation of the Prime Minister
and Foreign Secretary.

Why not a current civil servant?

It is perfectly reasonable for political appointments to be made to diplomatic roles - there is previous
precedent for this.

On background: Obviously this is a very important time for the UK-US relationship and Lord Mandelson is a
highly respected and experienced figure who will bring the required skills to the role as we build
relationships with the new administration and continue to strengthen our partnership.

What will he be paid?

0]

Has he giyen up his business interests?

His appointment will be subject to the usual processes which are ongoing, including declarations and any
requisite advice and mitigations, as required.

OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE
Hi 
Colleagues may have a view on terminology but I think we are ‘ appointing’ , subject to 
the conclusion of bilateral processes.
Head of Mission roles are usually advertised across Government. The FCDO's 
recruitment processes are underpinned by the principle of selection for appointment 
on merit, on the basis of fair and open competition. Recommendations for 
appointment for these roles are ratified by the Foreign Secretary and, for the most 
senior posts, the Prime Minister. Appointments are then approved by HMTK.
The Constitutional Reform and Governance (CRAG) Act 2010 is the statutory basis 
for ensuring that Civil Service recruitment is fair, open and based on merit. Section 10 
(3) of the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act allows for Head of Mission
appointments to take place outside of the CSC’ s rules and allows for political
appointments to be made to Head of Mission roles (or Governorships). Such
appointments are few in number but have been used by successive administrations
since 1945.
Best wishes
Rochelle

Subject: HMA-designate Washington - draft Press Announcement

OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE
Dear All,

Lord Mandelson is due to take up his posting as the next Ambassador to the United
States in January 2025 in succession to Dame Karen Pierce.
HRD Senior Appointments have informed me that Third Party

and Lord Mandelson's is in progress. As we may not hear anything further
until the New Year, there is a chance this will hold up the issue of Lord Mandelson’ s
contract.
Our normal announcing process is to prepare and clear a standard draft
announcement with the incumbent HoM, HoM-designate and geographical
department, ask when they would like the announcement to be made, and if there is a
need for wider clearance (through Special Advisers). We then clear the whole
announcement and the timing with the PUS's Office, and then send it to Newsdesk to
announce on the agreed date. We ask the HoM-designate to send us a suitable photo
as the announcement cannot be made without a photo.
If the draft announcement has to be cleared with No 10, this could delay things so |
thought it best to warn you. Karen Pierce's announcement wasn't in the standard
format and was done by Americas/Comms with heavy involvement from No 10
https:/Awww.gov.uk/government/news/karen-pierce-appointed-as-her-majestys-
ambassado! e-united-states-of-america so we expect Lord Mandelson's will be
similar especially given that his cv is non-standard.
Please let me know if you need anything further from me at this stage. As normal, the
announcement shouldn't be made until agrément has been granted (though this is a
moot point given the PM has already announced it).
Kind Regards,

JCS
JCS | Overseas Team - People Data Management, HR Services, Foreign,
Commonwealth & Development Office | Personal | Mobile: Persona!

HR Services are continually working to improve - you can help by telling us what you
think

Lord Mandelson & Minister Vallance 
Date: 11 September 2024 
Attendees: Minister Vallance (PV), Lord Mandelson (PM) 
PM opening remarks 
� 
PM noted potential plans for an industrial strategy council and highlighted past 
experience with xWH initiatives of this sort after the 2008 crisis. PM added that 
he views the work on the S&T Framework as aligned with this approach to 
governance. 
� 
PM added that he hopes R&D spending won't come under pressure and isn't 
clear on whether or not this government will protect it in the same way as may 
have been done in the past. 
o
PV noted examples in the private sector that show maintaining R&D
spend is important even in challenging times.
� 
PM brieﬂy mentioned that the new CEO of UKRI, when appointed, should be 
working within direction/a policy framework from government. 
PV opening remarks 
� 
PV highlighted the importance of a joined up approach regarding procurement in 
relation to R&D spending, as this is important for company growth. 
DSIT work broadly. 
� 
PM noted the work of DSIT on being the digital centre of government and on AI. 
� 
PV mentioned that DSIT is to play a very important role in these areas among 
others. 
S&T Framework 
� 
PV noted that the S&T Framework is still important and PV hopes there will be 
xWH collaboration across the diﬀerent areas that S&T crosses over. PV added the 
importance of Prime Ministerial authority and a driver in the centre to ensure 
this, and brieﬂy referred to the Cabinet Committee on S&T.

Dear Mr. Brian Davidson.
On behalf of Centre on Contemporary China and the World (CCCW) at
the University of Hong Kong (HKU) Director Li Cheng, I am delighted to
extend an exclusive invitation to you for a captivating guest talk with
Lord Peter Mandelson, where we will delve into the new era of British
foreign policy and its implications for Sino-UK relations and the global
landscape. The event will be available to the public for online viewing, but
the in-person event is invite-only.
Drawing from his extensive experience as a former European Trade
Commissioner and British First Secretary of State, Lord Mandelson will
provide invaluable insights on several key topics, including:
- Sir Keir Starmer's foreign policy
- The Labour Party's priorities in relation to China
- Strategies for businesses navigating geopolitical risks
- The role of higher education in fostering global cooperation
This event promises to be an enlightening experience, offering a unique
perspective from one of the foremost experts in the field. Your presence
would greatly enrich the discussion and add to the prestige of the
occasion.
Please find the event details below, and on the attached invitation:
Date: September 16, 2024
Time: 17:00-18:30
Location: Social Sciences Chamber, 11/F, The Jockey Club
Tower, Centennial Campus, HKU
We would be honoured to have you join us for this special event. Kindly
RSVP by Monday, September 9, to confirm your attendance. This
invitation is transferable, pending approval by CCCW.
We look forward to welcoming you.
Best,
Assistant to the Director
Centre on Contemporary China and the World (CCCW)
The University of Hong Kong
Follow us online: www.gov.uk/fcdo
Third Party

From: Peter Mandelson 
 
Sent: 13 September 2024 13:05 
To:
 
Cc:
 
 
Subject: Invitation to roundtable 
Dear Sarah, 
I hope you are well and settling into your important new role. 
I am planning to reschedule the roundtable you agreed to participate in just before the 
election was called - this time focussing on how the Clean Energy Mission can be an 
important lever for building out our industrial strategy . And I wondered if you would be 
willing and available to lead off a discussion on the topic? SSE will also give some initial 
remarks. 
A number of industry people have highlighted to me that the new government's more 
ambitious decarbonisation agenda presents a major economic opportunity to onshore cable 
manufacturing, leverage the UK's geographical strengths in carbon capture and repurpose 
the UK's ports for offshore wind turbine making . The challenge will be identifying where the 
UK can compete with its international counterparts on green manufacturing and ensuring 
that policy and regulation choices develop these opportunities. In the absence of this, the UK 
risks not fully capture the benefits of decarbonisation. 
I am keen to bring together a group of interesting and, hopefully, lively people to discuss this 
opportunity and challenge. I would plan to invite a mix of industry, Labour MPs and policy 
people, senior union people and relevant influencers for what would be a Chatham House 
rule discussion. 
Personal

the departments. 
I would be pleased to speak with you about if further if useful. We are potentially looking at 
late afternoon (votes permitting) on Tuesday 10th or Wednesday 11th December – might 
either work with your diary? My office can come back with some alternatives if not. 
I very much hope you can do this, and it would be great to hear what you have to say. 
Very best,

On 25 Jul 2024, at 10:47, Vallance, Minister (DSIT) Personal wrote:

163

JCS
Private Secretary to Lord Vallance | Department for Science, Innovation and
Technology| Personal

From: Peter Mandelson Personal
Sent: Thursday, July 25, 2024 10:08 AM

To: Vallance, Minister (DSIT) Personal
Subject: Economic change

Dear Patrick,

It was nice to see you last night. | am so pleased you are at DSIT. It is going to be a lean, mean
department where you can make a real difference.

The new government is now trying to do on stilts what we attempted to do 1997-10. In the two
bookends of my ministerial career, in 1998 and 2008, we tried to make a fundamental difference
to the growth and productivity drivers of the UK economy, primarily by changing and
strengthening the supply side of the economy — education and skills, transport and
infrastructure, university expansion and getting the right business and investment environment —
but latterly, too, by giving the government some skin in the game in bringing about investment in
commercialising the UK's science and technology base.

The 1998 white paper Building a knowledge-driven economy set about replicating in Britain the
west coast ecosystem of research/development/translation/commercialisation. It was based on
principles of competition, collaboration and clusters of new technology-based industry around
the country.

In 2008, we created the National Economic Council which met monthly in Cobr under Gordon's
chairmanship to galvanise everything the public and private sectors could throw at saving and
regenerating the UK economy following the banking crisis. This was what | was brought back
from Brussels to do in government and the white paper New industry, new jobs (Ninja) set out
how to do it.

| feel that Keir's government is trying to achieve a similar thing all over again but | am not aware
of any granular attempt to work out how better to do so based on previous experience. You
come closest to it but Michael Barber focused on public services not the economy (and will be
relevant to other government missions) and | am not sure what Nick Boles brings.

The truth is that we did not pull off what we attempted to do. We made a difference,
undoubtedly, but did we bring about structural change and fundamentally transform the
underlying performance of the economy? No. The machinery and performance of the
govemment was not up to that job. Although | could point to a number of things the Treasury
allowed us to take forward in my department which | agreed with Alistair Darling we were not
really all singing from the same hymn sheet or joined up in our thinking and actions and, most
importantly, we did not have scale.

The only attempts since seem to be a mix of Greg Clark's short-lived challenges and the
‘missions’ approach you set out to the Prime Minister (Johnson ? Sunak ?) and then of course
your vaccine task force. This was a very specific thing which Whitehall tends to do well when
mobilised. | would love to think you are having success in making a second run but my fear is
that Rachel's growth plan, Jonny's industrial strategy, Angela's planning and devolution reforms
and DSIT’s science drive are not really joined up. There are such big bets and trade offs to be
made if wishful thinking is to be turned into meaningful, sustainable change. The work | have
been doing for UUK on research and innovation has revealed to me that while we could always
spend and invest more on science, we are not getting full economic value from what we are
spending already and that UKRI, for all its strengths, hasn't the heft and punch to make the
really big calls, notwithstanding Andrew Mackenzie's effective leadership.

In policy and narrative, as | said to you, the government is over reliant on building houses and
energy infrastructure as the golden route forward. These can help but there are limits to their
impact on the economy's underlying productivity, ability to generate new businesses, establish
UK supply chains and increase exports.

We have started well in the UK in Al but | am less convinced we will finish sufficiently ahead.
Ditto bioscience and quantum. The main reason is not government policy, relevant as that is,
but the lack of really big UK private sector companies driving the necessary advance and
competitiveness. Will the proposed Industrial Strategy Council make the transformative
difference in deploying the business acumen, finance capital and human talent in the teams and
networks of our universities and private sector ? The thinking so far would not suggest it.
Labour has, let's say, two terms to succeed. So far Keir’s team has not prioritised policy (not
surprising given how much else had to be re-built after Corbyn). And the No 10 policy team is
not large or experienced. Pat at the Cabinet Office will have the right instincts but | am an
afficionado of the Cabinet Office (I started there as Minister without Portfolio and ended there as
First Secretary of State) and it is a light touch co-ordinator not a power house.

| know you will be thinking every day how to make a difference. | wish you all possible luck !

164


Very best

Peter

Lord Mandelson

Chairman

A: 5 Welbeck Street, London, W1G 9YQ
Tt Personal

166

The new government is now trying to do on stilts what we attempted to do 1997-10. In the two bookends of 
my ministerial career, in 1998 and 2008, we tried to make a fundamental difference to the growth and 
productivity drivers of the UK economy, primarily by changing and strengthening the supply side of the 
economy - education and skills, transport and infrastructure, university expansion and getting the right 
business and investment environment - but latterly, too, by giving the government some skin in the game in 
bringing about investment in commercialising the UK's science and technology base. 
The 1998 white paper Building a knowledge-driven economy set about replicating in Britain the west coast 
ecosystem of research/developmenUtranslation/commercialisation. It was based on principles of 
competition, collaboration and clusters of new technology-based industry around the country. 
In 2008, we created the National Economic Council which met monthly in Cobr under Gordon's 
chairmanship to galvanise everything the public and private sectors could throw at saving and regenerating 
the UK economy following the banking crisis. This was what I was brought back from Brussels to do in 
government and the white paper New industry, new jobs (Ninja) set out how to do it. 
I feel that Keir's government is trying to achieve a similar thing all over again but I am not aware of any 
granular attempt to work out how better to do so based on previous experience. You come closest to it but 
Michael Barber focused on public services not the economy (and will be relevant to other Keir missions) and 
I am not sure what Nick Boles brings. 
The truth is that we did not pull off what we attempted to do. We made a difference, undoubtedly, but did we 
bring about structural change and fundamentally transform the underlying performance of the economy? 
No. The machinery and performance of the government was not up to that job. Although I could point to a 
number of things the Treasury allowed us to take forward in my department which I agreed with Alistair 
Darling we were not really all singing from the same hymn sheet or joined up in our thinking and actions and 
we did not have scale. 
The only attempts since seem to be a mix of Greg Clark's short-lived challenges and the 'missions' 
approach you set out to the Prime Minister (Johnson ? Sunak ?) and then of course your vaccine task force. 
This was a very specific thing which Whitehall tends to do well when mobilised. I would love to think you are 
having success in making a second run but my fear is that Rachel's growth plan, Jonny's industrial strategy, 
Angela's planning and devolution reforms and DSIT's science drive are not really joined up. There are such 
big bets and trade offs to be made if wishful thinking is to be turned into meaningful, sustainable change. 
The work I have been doing for UUK on research and innovation has revealed to me that while we could 
always spend and invest more on science, we are not getting full economic value from what we are 
spending already and that UKRI, for all its strengths, hasn't the heft and punch to make the really big calls, 
notwithstanding Andrew Mackenzie's strong leadership. 
In policy and narrative, as I said to you, the government is over reliant on building houses and energy 
infrastructure as the golden route forward. These can help but there are limits to their impact on the 
economy's underlying productivity, ability to generate new businesses, establish UK supply chains and 
increase exports. 
We have started well in the UK in Al but I am less convinced we will finish sufficiently ahead. Ditto 
bioscience and quantum. The main reason is not government policy, relevant as that is, but the lack of 
really big UK private sector companies driving the necessary advance and competitiveness. Will the 
proposed Industrial Strategy Council make the transformative difference in deploying the business acumen, 
finance capital and human talent in the teams and networks of our universities and private sector ? The 
thinking so far would not suggest it. 
Labour has, let's say, two terms to succeed. So far Keir's team has not prioritised policy (not surprising given 
how much else had to be re-built after Corbyn). And the No 10 policy team is not large or experienced. Pat 
at the Cabinet Office will have the right instincts but I am an afficionado of the Cabinet Office (I started there 
as Minister without Portfolio and ended there as First Secretary of State) and it is a light touch co-ordinator 
not a power house. 
I know you will be thinking every day how to make a difference. I wish you all possible luck ! 
Very best 
Peter

description: 56 - 13-01-2025 - Meetings between Peter Mandelson and Michael Ellam (Second Permanent Secretary, European Union and International Economic Affairs, CO)

participants.name: Meetings between Peter Mandelson and Michael Ellam
participants.role: Second Permanent Secretary, European Union and International Economic Affairs

text:
Permanent Secretary, European Union and International Economic Affairs, CO)

Meetings with former HMA Washington

13 January: Michael/ MCO/ Peter- This was a 30-minute introductory meeting between the Minister for the Cabinet
Office, the new HMA and Michael. As the responsible minister for the European Union Relations Secretariat, the
purpose of this meeting was for MCO to set out HMG ambition for resetting the European relationship and how the
former Ambassador might be able to support this from Washington.

17 January: Michael/Peter—this was an informal official-level lunch within 70 Whitehall. It was an hour long
meeting for Michael and HMA to discuss the incoming US administration and their own priorities for building
relations in Brussels and Washington respectively. As this was an informal lunch before HMA flew to Washington
there was no readout.

20 Febuary- 28 Feburary: US Tariffs Check in- At the onset of tariff announcements from the new US
administration our office organised daily tariff check in calls, the cast list of these calls varied but included DBT
Secretary of State (SoS), National Security Advisor (NSA), the Prime Minister's Chief of Staff, the Prime Minister's
Foreign Affairs Permanent Secretary (PS), the Prime Minister's Business Advisor, Deputy National Security
Advisors (DNSAs), the Permanent Secretary for DBT, and other working-level officials. The initial calls

were on Switch before moving to MS Teams- there weren't specific readouts but the calls were to give daily updates
from Washington and Whitehall.

17 April: Varun/ Kate Joseph/ Peter - Following "Liberation Day" there was a small call between DBT/ No10
and Cabinet Office to coordinate HMG response to the tariff announcement, update of the reaction in Washington
and various capitals across Europe. There was no read out from this meeting.

13 May: Michael/ Peter - On the evening of the 13th May Michael had a brief 15 minute phone call with HMA to
reassure him of the package agreed with the EU for the upcoming summit (19 May) would have no consequences
on the EPD that was concurrently being negotiated with the US. No readout of this meeting.

24 June: Michael/ Peter - The former Ambassador was in London to attend a DBT away day; he was speaking

to the away day virtually from a room in Cabinet Office. As a result Michael and the former Ambassador agreed
to a quick 15 minute catch up ahead of HMA speaking at the DBT event. No readout.

JCS | PPS to the Second Permanent

Secretary European and Global Issues | Personal

183

description: 57 - 14-01-2025 - Meeting between Peter Mandelson and Pat McFadden (former Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster and Minister for Intergovernmental Relations)
text:
Duchy of Lancaster and Minister for Intergovernmental Relations)

Lord Mandelson readout

Hi all,

CDL met with the HM Ambassador-Designate to the United States, Lord Mandelson, on

Tuesday 14 January. Please find a readout below and cascade as appropriate.

Attendees:
e Lord Mandelson, HMA-Designate to the US
. Jes CDL’s Office

Actions:

e CDL referenced an article by Robert Kagan on Russia/Ukraine and asked that this
was shared with Lord Mandelson. I've linked it here and passed it on to Lord
Mandelson’s team.

CDL and Lord Mandelson discussed the implications for the change of administration for the
UK’s relationship with the US. Lord Mandelson noted that the Government should approach
the relationship with an intensely relaxed approach, rather than being concerned with optics
or gestures.

CDL updated on the PM Small Strategy Group on the US, including the Government's aims,
and that the position of the UK outside major trading blocks was both vulnerable and agile.
There were significant trade opportunities with China that would not cause national security
concerns. However, the UK’s relationship with China would be affected by the overarching
dynamics of the US-China relationship. The Government had room to manoeuvre its audit to
make a decision that was in the UK national interest, but the implementation was as
important as the strategy itself.

Trade negotiations with the US were discussed, where the incoming administration would
likely focus on agriculture, especially considering the support required from Congress and
the farming lobby. CDL reaffirmed his support for the UK farming sector, and his desire not to
expose them to unfair competition.

The Russia/Ukraine war was also discussed, touching on the implications of different
outcomes of the conflict on the geopolitical position of key partners.

[ends]

184

Good afternoon,  
Please find a readout from SoSNI's meeting with Lord Mandelson below: 
Readout: SoSNI meeting with Lord Mandelson 
23 January 2025, NIO London   
Attendees: 
Secretary of State for Northern Ireland (SoSNI), Lord Peter Mandelson (Lord Mandelson) Will Gelling, MaryJo 
McKeever, 
, 
, 
Overview:   
SoSNI had a conversation with Lord Mandelson ahead of his appointment as the UK Ambassador to the US. They 
discussed the St Patrick’s Day events in Washington, and offered an update on NI politics and the progression 
around Legacy.   
Readout: 
1. SoSNI began by congratulating Lord Mandelson and asking when he expected to be appointed, Lord Mandelson
said in 2 weeks.
2. Lord Mandelson asked about President Trump's views on NI. SoSNI said he had not spoken about NI publicly,
3. SoSNI updated Lord Mandelson on the political situation in NI, he explained this had been a very tricky time for
both Gavin Robinson and Unionism in general. He explained that there was some optimism from the Unionist
politicians around a Trump Presidency, and both Ian Paisley Jr and Baroness Hoey travelled to Washington for
the inauguration.
4.
Lord Mandelson asked about

5. SoSNI updated Lord Mandelson on progress around Legacy. He explained the commitment to repeal and
replace, but stressed he did not want to stop the ICRIR only to replace it with a similar body and delay cases
being investigated. SoSNI said he ordered an inquiry into the murder of Patrick Finucane 
, but had been ordered by a Judge to conduct a public inquiry into Brown
which the UKG was challenging.
6. SoSNI and Lord Mandelson discussed St Patrick’s Day events in Washington DC. SoSNI said he was looking
forward to the engagements but was awaiting finalised timings for the key events. Lord Mandelson asked if he
expected all the events to go ahead given the new administration, SoSNI said he believed they would.
Actions:   
No actions following the meeting. 
Best wishes,

description: 82 - 14-02-2025 - Letter from Peter Mandelson to David Lammy (former Secretary of State for Foreign Commonwealth and Development Affairs:
text:
Commonwealth and Development Affairs:

From the Ambassador

The Rt Hon Lord Mandelson PC
His Majesty’s Ambassador

British Embassy atingin Oe ante
Washington re. Personal

14 February 2025

The Rt Hon David Lammy MP
Secretary of State for Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Affairs

fod,

The View from Washington: Priorities for the Special Relationship

In my first week as the British Ambassador to the US, | wanted to write to you to set out my
vision and ambition as | currently see it — for my role, for that of the Embassy, and for how |,
the Embassy in Washington and the whole of the US network work with Whitehall.

In order to deliver key aspects of the Prime Minister’s plan for change, and your six stated

priorities, my overarching goal is to refresh and revivify the basis of the UK's partnership
with the US. This partnership should recognise that we are

228

| would like the work of the Embassy and network to reflect these priorities, and for us to be
in a space to assist in shaping HMG’s current and future policy-making. To do this, the
Embassy's top team will work in a collective way with the whole government, just as!
expect HMG will want to do with us on US policy-making.

This approach will, however, also require trade-offs. In all the areas cited above, what

Alliance. This will be reflected in the Government’s new approach to the EU

British Ambassador to the United States

description: 83 - 21-02-2025 - Email from Peter Mandelson to Matthew Doyle (former Director of
text:
Communications, No.10) and Morgan McSweeney (former Chief of Staff to the Prime

Minister, No.10)

From: Peter Mandelson|Personal

Sent: Fri 21/02/2025 6:31:35 PM (UTC)

To: Matthew DoylePersonal
MMcSweeneyPersonal

Subject: Bullets for media

* The PM's visit comes at a moment of tension within the NATO alliance over Ukraine
but also, in his view, a time of great economic opportunity between the Uk and US.

* PM believes he can be a bridge between EU and US views re Ukraine because he
strongly believes that the only viable outcome of any negotiation has to be a permanent
peace not a temporary ceasefire but he also acknowledges that, to get Russia to the table,
President Trump's relationship with Putin has to be utilised and that only the US has the
heft in Putin's eyes to get talks started.

* The PM is confident of his personal relationship with President Trump. He has
deliberately used his recent telephone calls with him to establish a strong personal bond
rather than take on wider policy issues. But the meeting this week will draw on the strong
personal relations they have established to reach convergence on key policy questions.

* Current issues are Chagos and tariffs. On the former, the US has made certain asks of
detail in relation to the deal that sees the US retaining full operational use of Diego Garcia
with China kept firmly at bay. PM is hopeful that US concerns will be accommodated in
continuing talks with Mauritius. And on tariffs, PM will be citing Britain's existing
balanced and fair trading relationship with the US in order to contain any immediate
threat of tariffs. However, the PM recognises the President's strength of feeling on trade
and he knows that there are some tough discussions yet to be had if Britain is to avoid
being indirectly damaged by growing trade frictions between the EU and the US.

+ Alongside their discussion about Ukraine and the related future of NATO spending, the
PM wants to make a new economic growth deal the centrepiece of the meeting. He wants
the outcome to be agreement on the creation of a full spectrum technology partnership
rooted in shared scientific discovery, innovation, investment and exchange of talent. This
already exists in the defence industrial sphere where there is considerable technology
sharing and large potential for further development but more widely in artificial
intelligence and also in quantum computing, human and life sciences, space, advanced
nuclear and bioscience.

* The PM wants to lose no time in advancing and agreeing this economic and technology
partnership and wants it to come together this year as a key part of the government's
economic growth mission.

* The PM is absolutely convinced that, while a reset in EU relations is vital, the potential
for the US-UK relationship to make both countries safer and stronger is enormous. He has
great regard for the President's ambitions for the US and the way in which he has hit the
ground running and wants Britain to be not only America's pre-eminent security ally but
its pre-eminent critical technology partner as well. He sees the meeting as a take off point
for both and will come to Washington with considerable enthusiasm and ambition for
what can be achieved.

230

. Exact timing will depend on speakers before and after

description: 87 - 24-02-2025 - Meeting between Peter Mandelson and James Bowler (Permanent Secretary to the Treasury, HMT
text:
to the Treasury, HMT

From: Jcs

Sent: 24 February 2026 17:37
To: Crisp, Jonathan - HMT

ce Jcs

Subject: James B / Peter M readout
Follow Up Flag: Follow up

Flag Status: Completed

29" January 2025: James Bowler (JB) and Lord Madleson (LM)

e On business sentiment, LM said markets, businesses and investors, etc. gave the Government
significant goodwill on entering office, but he felt they lacked a clearly articulated, deep-seated
“growth philosophy”. He thought the business community had not been expecting what was in the
Budget in the autumn. He accepted the fiscal correction was necessary, but this had come at the
expense of business and was counter to the pre-election narrative. JB set out that Covid and energy
interventions meant difficult choices had to be made.

JCS | Private Secretary to Jim O’Neil, Second Permanent Secretary

Washington =f i

24 February 2025

The Rt Hon Sir Keir Starmer KCB KC MP
Prime Minister

Dear Prime Minister,

VISIT TO WASHINGTON - 27 FEBRUARY 2025

President Trump has brought his particular stamp to the White House. America First
is the lodestar of his Administration. Policy decisions and approaches flow from that.
Over the next four years his deeply loyal and effective administration will play the
international system by a different set of rules — impacting trade, foreign policy, and
domestic nolicv with international implications. 8%

Trumn’s first month has seen his Administration *

|

H At his inauguration President
Trump said he intended to deliver on the promises made during his campaign,
especially on immigration, reform of the Federal government, and tariffs and trade
relationships. In the month since assuming office, his Administration has followed
through on this with amazing vigour.

02- 26-02-2025- Letter to Peter Mandelson from John Healey (Secre' of State for Defence

SECRETARY OF STATE

MINISTRY OF DEFENCE

MAIN BUILDING, WHITEHALL

ini LONDON, SW1A 2HB

Ministry Telephone: Personal )

of Defence Personal c
4-5-1-2 DOFebruary 2025

Thank you for your letter dated 20" February asking for views on what an ambitious
UK-US defence industrial and technology partnership should focus on.

| agree that there is merit in developing a bold pitch to the US which supports our
emerging Strategic Defence Review (SDR) and Defence Industrial Strategy (DIS)
providing a tangible connection between the economic and security aspects of our
partnership with the new US administration. The UK spends 43% of total spend on
defence industry overseas with the US, with a US Foreign Military Sales (FMS)
pipeline of $18Bn, which excludes major programmes such as F35, Trident and
MLRS (which the UK invests heavily in under different arrangements). And there
are substantial opportunities for the UK if we can attract more US investment — the
six largest US defence investors currently support over 100,000 jobs in the UK and
have over 7,000 British companies in their supply chain.

Defence is an engine that drives economic growth, providing the national security
that is a pre-condition to economic security, investor confidence and social stability.
When the Prime Minister is in Washington later this week, he might look to set out
our ambition to work with the US on defence industries of the future, with companies
— such as Palantir and Anduril — already driving productivity and innovation through
initiatives such as ‘warp speed’. This a sector that leverages cutting-edge
technologies, such as Al and autonomous systems, catalysing economic
development through positive spill-over effects. Our success in this rests on a new
partnership with domestic and international innovators, investors and industry and
is fundamental to both our long-term security and economic growth.

| have instructed officials to work at pace to examine how we can strengthen our
partnership with the US on these issues with a focus on three key pillars.

First, on R&D and Innovation. The US is our partner of choice for advanced
research and technology and which strongly benefits both of our nations. We have
agreed with US partners at official level that — i ‘ \ 1
: ‘ i i i i |
| , ,

Lord Peter Mandelson
HMA Washington

309

 We need 
now to supercharge our efforts to ensure that we can innovate and experiment -
with allies - at the pace of the threat. The SOR and the DIS will set out how we are 
reforming the Ministry of Defence to ensure we can get after this better. As part of 
that work, I have directed officials to work to develop options for further collaboration 
with the US, particularly arou joint testing and experimentation, and bring forward 
projects where there is the greatest shared national security and economic benefit. 
Successful implementation will be a vital 
component in driving greater innovation, speed, and industrial collaboration, 
unlocking more license-free technology and information transfer. 
In developing these proposals, we will need to carefully consider the balance 
between our partnership with the US and that with our European allies. We will also 
need to reassure stakeholders that this work is not at the expense of jobs in either 
the UK or the US but is essential to grow the sector for our shared benefit. The work 
should also be conscious of, and in alignment with, our NATO commitments, 
Lord Peter Mandelson 
HMA Washington 
***
***
***
310


should also be conscious of, and in alignment with, our NATO commitments,
recognising the need for increased European burden sharing to provide the
necessary support for Ukraine.

Yours sincerely,
PERSONAL ‘

THE RT HON Y MP

Secretary of State for Defence

Lord Peter Mandelson
HMA Washington

Hi
Not for forwarding.
Can I have your views on this? Our sense is that the advice below is sensible 
Ailsa
OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE
From: Eleanor Mackewn 
Sent: 06 March 2025 11:20
To: Ninjeri Pandit ; Ailsa Terry ; PUS Action ; 
 ; Victoria Busby ; 
 (Protocol) 
Subject: Urgent: Advice for DssE visit to NYC
OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE - HMG ONLY
All,
The Duchess of Edinburgh will be New York next week to coincide with the 69th Session 
of the UN Commission on the Status of Women (CSW). Her role as patron for the NGO 
PATH provided the original hook for the visit. It was agreed with Buckingham Palace in 
late January that HRH was permitted to add additional serials to her programme to 
support HMG objectives. These additional programme elements are all closely aligned 
to the Duchess' long-standing advocacy for work preventing Conflict Related Sexual 
Violence, and women's rights more broadly (including in other geographies, such as 
Afghanistan). The King's Principal Private Secretary has asked Royal Unit to 
provide the PUS, PPS/PM and PS/PM advice on the programme, seeking an urgent 
steer on whether the FCDO and Number 10 believe the visit should proceed (or be 
amended).
The Ukrainian programme components are 
:
•
The first is HRH speak at a meeting of the International Alliance on PSVI. The UK
created the alliance in 2023, but Ukraine is the Chair in 2025. The DPM of Ukraine will
host the meeting next week, though the Duchess’ intervention is planned to focus on her
visit to Chad, not Ukraine. FCDO officials believe this event is not contentious and hope
the Duchess is permitted to attend and speak about Chad.

We discussed – I have changed below to ensure it matches the core four points the 
PM wanted to make at PMQs in order to sound as active and confident as possible. 
Welcome views and amends to make the language more user friendly, but hopefully 
the substance makes sense. @Michael Ellam @Varun Chandra shout if anything 
goes further than you would be comfortable with on the talks.
DBT SoS statement – to be issued from Wednesday 12th March, 4am GMT
Business and Trade Secretary Jonathan Reynolds said:
“It's disappointing the US has today imposed global tariffs on steel and aluminium.
“We are taking a pragmatic approach and a trade war is in nobody’s interests
We are focused on rapidly negotiating a wider economic agreement with the US 
to eliminate additional tariffs and benefit UK business and our economy
Meanwhile we remain resolute in our support for UK industry. This Government is 
working with affected companies today, and I back industry’s application to the 
Trade Remedies Authority to investigate what further steps might be necessary to 
protect UK producers.
I will continue to engage closely and pragmatically with the US to press the case for 
UK business interests. We will keep all options on the table, and won’t hesitate to 
respond in the national interest.
ENDS
Are you going to retaliate?
The tariffs on steel and aluminium proposed by the Trump Administration are global, 
not targeted at the UK.
We are looking at all options, but we won’t be imposing tariffs immediately.
Let’s be clear. Industry doesn’t want to see a trade war where both sides keep 
escalating the situation. Standing up for industry means finding a solution.
Our approach to the US is pragmatic and we continue to have productive discussions 
on tariffs and other issues.
We have raised this with the US at various levels to make the case for UK companies 
to be exempt from US global tariffs and will continue to do so through our productive 
conversations.
You aren’t retaliating now but why aren’t you retaliating now? Other countries are.
• We are taking a pragmatic approach in seeking a wider economic deal. A trade war
is in nobody’s interests.
JCS
360


• We will keep all options on the table, but our focus is securing a deal that will benefit
industry and the British people.
Isn’t this a pretty weak response?
• I wouldn’t accept that – this is a cool-headed and pragmatic response.
• We rule nothing out and we will always act in the best interests of the British people
and economy.
• But a knee-jerk response today won’t help us achieve a long-term economic deal
with the US tomorrow, and that is our focus.
Why no exemption? PM failed at diplomacy?
We have raised this with the US at various levels to make the case for UK companies 
to be exempt from US global tariffs.
The Prime Minister has raised steel and aluminium tariffs with President Trump - in a 
call on Monday and when he was at the White House in February. The Business and 
Trade Secretary has also raised this with his counterpart Secretary Lutnick.
Our focus is on reaching a wider economic deal will benefit the industry and the 
British people.
Doesn’t say much for PM’s relationship with POTUS if they spoke and Trump ignored 
him?
• It is because of the Prime Minister’s successful visit to the White House that UK and
US officials are in talks about a future economic deal that will benefit businesses on
both sides of the Atlantic.
• These wider global tariffs have come into effect while we are in the middle of those
negotiations.
• We remain focused on that future trading partnership. We already have a fair and
balanced trade relationship and are the largest single investors in one another’s
economies, so there is plenty we can build on.
Is the industry also now going to be hit by tariffs from Trump?
The tariffs on steel, aluminium and derivatives being proposed by the Trump 
administration are global, not targeted at the UK.
We will continue to take a pragmatic approach and remain prepared to defend the 
UK's national interest where it is right to do so.
EU and Canada have announced retaliatory tariffs, weak PM?
I don’ t accept that. Our approach to the US has been pragmatic and we continue to 
have productive conversations with the US administration at all levels.
Our focus is a long-term wider economic deal which will see benefits for the industry 
361


and hardworking British people.
How will these damage the steel industry?
We have been working closely with the steel and aluminium sectors over the last few 
weeks to understand what the expected impact of tariffs will be on industry and will 
continue to do so.
In 2023, the US accounted for 5% of UK steel exports and 6% of UK aluminium 
exports.
Whilst these aren’ t huge volumes, that does not mean we are not taking this extremely 
seriously and doing everything we can to try to avoid UK industry being hit.
What are you doing to protect the steel industry?
This government will simply not allow the end of steel making in the UK.
That’ s why we have committed up to £2.5 billion of investment to rebuild the UK 
steel industry and support communities now and for generations to come. 
And next month, the final measure included in the British Industry Supercharger 
package comes into force, bringing energy costs for important UK industries –  
including steel –  closer in line with other major economies around the world.
Once fully implemented, the total value of reduced electricity prices is expected to be 
between £320 million and £410 million this year, and over £5 billion over 10 years.
We also back the industry’ s application to the Trade Remedies Authority (TRA) 
investigate what further steps might be necessary to protect UK producers.
If needed:
Only 5% of UK steel exports and 6% of aluminium exports (by volume) go to the US 
(worth noting it’ s high value, so a higher % by value).
The current steel safeguard is critical to protect our industry against unfair trading 
practices and was recently extended to June 2026.
This was designed to protect from the inflow of steel diverted from the US Market 
when it was put in place in 2018. Through the steel strategy, we will ensure that there 
is a plan for our critical steel industry following the expiration of the Safeguard.
How are we managing the risks to businesses in Northern Irelad?
We will always act in the best interests of all UK businesses which of course includes those 
in Northern Ireland.
As I have said, whilst we are not considering imposing tariffs immediately, we absolutely 
reserve the right to do so if necessary.
362


As per the Windsor Framework, where goods do not subsequently enter the EU, the Duty 
Reimbursement Scheme enables traders to reclaim or remit EU applicable duties in full 
without any limit on total claims.
We will look closely at any retaliatory tariffs announced by the EU any impact these might 
have on businesses, but it is our understanding these will not come into effect immediately 
[guide 31 March if public].
Will you revive UK tariffs on US products including whiskey, jeans and 
motorbikes?
All options are on the table so it’ s one option, but we won’ t be imposing tariffs 
immediately.
We’ re prioritising engagement with the US to stand up for industry and find a 
resolution. We will continue to take a cool-headed approach and reserve our right to 
retaliate, remaining prepared to defend the UK's national interest.
What engagement are you doing?
Industry Minister Sarah Jones met senior representatives from the steel and aluminium 
sectors yesterday and the Secretary of State speak to UK Steel today.
As you will have seen yesterday, the Trade and Economics policy manager at UK 
steel said that making the UK’ s steel exempt from the tariffs is a “ key priority”  and 
that DBT is “ all over it” .
Some steel companies have said you should make preparations to implement 
emergency tariffs should there be evidence of import surges in the coming 
months. Are you?
UK Steel has applied to the TRA for a functional review of the steel safeguard 
measure in light of the US tariffs, as well as broader challenges posed by global 
overcapacity in the steel market, and I [the Business and Trade Secretary] back that 
application.
Market reaction?
As you know we do not comment on markets.
How are we managing the risks to businesses in Northern Irelad?
We will always act in the best interests of all UK businesses which of course includes 
those in Northern Ireland.
As I have said, whilst we are not considering imposing tariffs immediately, we 
absolutely reserve the right to do so if necessary.
As per the Windsor Framework, where goods do not subsequently enter the EU, the 
Duty Reimbursement Scheme enables traders to reclaim or remit EU applicable duties 
363


in full without any limit on total claims.
We will look closely at any retaliatory tariffs announced by the EU any impact these 
might have on businesses, but it is our understanding these will not come into effect 
immediately [guide 31 March if public].
What trade protections are in place for the steel and aluminium industries?
The UK currently applies 44 trade remedy measures in addition to the globally applied 
steel safeguard measure.
We currently apply a global safeguard measure on 14 separate steel product 
categories, 17 separate anti-dumping or anti-subsidy measures on certain steel imports, 
and 2 measures on certain aluminium imports.  
The Trade Remedies Authority regularly discusses trade remedy measures with 
industry, and we encourage continued dialogue and any applications for new measures 
or the review of existing measures to ensure that UK businesses are protected from 
unfair foreign trade practices.
Next steps to securing a trade deal? Timelines?
Getting the right deal is the most important thing, but we are working at pace with the 
US to deliver this.
We are already having regular dialogue with each other.
Why are you waiting until Spring to publish the Steel Strategy when industry 
needs action now?
We have committed to publish Our Plan for Steel, on which we’re currently 
consulting, in the Spring. It will look at long-term issues facing the sector such as high 
electricity costs, unfair trading practices and scrap metal recycling.
We want to move swiftly, but in a way which addresses the longstanding issues the 
sector has faced for years and in line with other key workstreams such as developing 
the Industrial Strategy and Trade Strategy.
This is not easy, but we are determined to succeed.
OFFICIAL
OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Business and Trade Secretary Jonathan Reynolds said:
“It's disappointing the US has today imposed global tariffs on steel and aluminium.
"The UK will always be a champion of free and open trade which is essential in 
delivering our Plan for Change.

affected companies today, and I back industry’s application to the Trade Remedies

so we have a bit of time to work out a response. Essentially as it stands, NI businesses 
importing steel/aluminium from the US will have to take on the cost as per the Windsor 
Framework which sees the free movement of goods between NI and Ireland –  do correct me 
if I am wrong here.
I think the below line works but let me know:
How are we managing the risks to businesses in Northern Irelad?
We will always act in the best interests of all UK businesses which of course includes those 
in Northern Ireland.
As I have said, whilst we are not considering imposing tariffs immediately, we absolutely 
reserve the right to do so if necessary.
As per the Windsor Framework, where goods do not subsequently enter the EU, the Duty 
Reimbursement Scheme enables traders to reclaim or remit EU applicable duties in full 
without any limit on total claims.
We will look closely at any retaliatory tariffs announced by the EU any impact these might 
have on businesses, but it is our understanding these will not come into effect immediately 
[guide 31 March if public].
US TARIFFS 20250312
ISSUE: The US global tariffs on steel and aluminium have come into effect.
RESPONSE:
It is disappointing to see the US has imposed global tariffs on steel and aluminium.
We absolutely reserve the right to impose retaliatory tariffs, and we will continue to 
have productive conversations with the US administration at all levels on this issue.
But our focus is on reaching a wider economic deal will benefit the industry and the 
British people.
Any action we take will be in the interest of UK businesses, consumers and the 
economy.
Response to tariffs?
The tariffs on steel and aluminium proposed by the Trump Administration are global, 
not targeted at the UK.
We are looking at all options, but we won’ t be imposing tariffs immediately.
Let’ s be clear. Industry doesn’ t want to see a trade war where both sides keep 
escalating the situation. Standing up for industry means finding a solution.
Our approach to the US is pragmatic and we continue to have productive discussions 
370


on tariffs and other issues.
We have raised this with the US at various levels to make the case for UK companies 
to be exempt from US global tariffs and will continue to do so through our productive 
conversations.
Why no exemption? PM failed at diplomacy?
We have raised this with the US at various levels to make the case for UK companies 
to be exempt from US global tariffs.
The Prime Minister has raised steel and aluminium tariffs with President Trump - in a 
call on Monday and when he was at the White House in February. The Business and 
Trade Secretary has also raised this with his counterpart Secretary Lutnick.
Our focus is on reaching a wider economic deal will benefit the industry and the 
British people.
Is the industry also now going to be hit by tariffs from Trump?
The tariffs on steel, aluminium and derivatives being proposed by the Trump 
administration are global, not targeted at the UK.
We will continue to take a pragmatic approach and remain prepared to defend the 
UK's national interest where it is right to do so.
For latest news and information from Downing Street visit: http://www.gov.uk/number10
Follow Number 10 on X (Twitter): http://x.com/10DowningStreet

British Embassy, Washington DC

Commonwealth & Development Office may be monitored in line with relevant UK legislation

OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

388

15 March 2025 
The Rt. Hon Jonathan Reynolds MP 
Secretary of State for Business and Trade 
Dear Jonny, 
Scenesetter: your visit to Washington DC, 17-18th March 
Your visit will demonstrate UK commitment to advancing the Prime Minister and President’ s 
economic and technology deal. It comes at a critical time after US tariffs on steel and aluminium and 
ahead of expected aggressive “reciprocal” tariffs on 2 April. Sectoral tariffs, e.g. on pharmaceuticals, 
semi-conductors, autos, are also coming down the track. 
This is not just about “equalising” America’ s trade relationships through addressing tariffs and non-
tariff barriers. The America First policy aims to rebuild domestic manufacturing and create a new 
global trade order with allies working closely together (following US rules) that leaves China 
increasingly on the “other side of the fence”.
From the Ambassador  
The Rt Hon Lord Mandelson PC 
His Majesty’ s Ambassador 
3100 Massachusetts Ave, NW 
Washington, DC  20008 
Tel:

description: 132 - 15-03-2025 - Letter from Peter Mandelson to Jonathan Reynolds  (former Secretary of State, DBT) Personal *** 389
text:
(former Secretary of State, DBT)
Personal
***
389

You and officials will be ready to present a clear and detailed offer on Tuesday. There are high 
expectations that the UK is coming with a serious proposal. You will need to speak to the full set of 
issues they care about as a collective 
It is great you are also seeing business groups and key CEOs. Congress is in recess, after reaching a 
deal on the US budget to avert a shutdown.  
I will be sorry to miss you, but I am committed to events on the West Coast with big tech. I look forward 
to hearing how the talks have gone and wish you all the best in what is a crucial endeavour for our 
country.  
Lord Mandelson 
British Ambassador to the United States 
Personal

***

for Science, Innovation and Technology)
