Sent: Sat 15/03/2025 12:31:52 PM (UTC)

To: Peter Kyle | Personal

Subject: Fw: HMA Washington: tech op-ed : next week Monday/Tuesday

FYI this is something written for a DC digital outlet as a place marker to start spreading
awareness of what we are trying to do in tech partnership.

Get Outlook for iOS

From: JCS

Sent: Friday, March 14, 2025 9:12:04 PM

To: Peter Mandelson

Subject: HMA Washington: tech op-ed : next week Monday/Tuesday

OFFICIAL

For Peter K

IN DRAFT HMA Lord Mandelson: tech op-ed

STARTS

Those who enjoyed Christopher Nolan’ s 2023 blockbuster Oppenheimer will recall the
intensive scientific collaboration and advancement - from Cambridge England to Los
Alamos New Mexico - that resulted in the allied powers winning the race to develop
the hydrogen bomb. Anglo-American partnership on this most lethal of deterrents
deepened further in 1962, when over three days in the Bahamas John F Kennedy and
Harold Macmillan mapped out the Polaris Sales Agreement, which to this day
underpins the joint nuclear umbrella the US and UK provide its people and its allies.
These historic technological partnerships — for all their destructive might — ultimately
allowed us to secure a technological edge over our adversaries and bring an end to
the tyranny and slaughter of the second world war. Thereafter, the joint nuclear
deterrent delivered an unprecedented period of stability for our nations that under-
wrote decades of rampant economic growth. An exemplar of peace through strength,
you might say.

American and British science cooperation — ever restless - went on to deliver the
technology upon which all contemporary commerce, communication and defence is
based; the internet.

We don’ t need another deterrent but there are major life enhancing and lifesaving
advances to which we need to put our minds. The biggest immediate example is Al.
We stand at the foothills of this next great foundation technology that will redefine the
century ahead, accelerating every other endeavour and discovery we want to

391

undertake.
I will join many of Britain’ s greatest AI innovators and investors this week in California 
for the Global Tech Conference, the world’ s premier AI gathering. Of the many 
issues we will discuss with our US and allied counterparts, I expect a central theme to 
dominate: the strategic technology rivalry between us and our adversaries.
Once more, we must combine our strengths to keep our citizens ahead in the global 
race. Our two science-rich nations, the US and UK, are the only two western countries 
with trillion-dollar technology eco-systems. Our combined research and innovation 
prowess - the US and UK are home to the top fifteen universities in the world - 
arguably remains our greatest defence against the values of freedom we cherish.
The challenges we face today are as profound as any we have confronted together in 
our past. Yet so are the opportunities.
While many governments are focussed on the risks and the dangers of AI’ s unknown 
powers, too few are investing enough energy to unlocking the enormity of life 
improving potential AI can unleash, from health, to education, to the security of our 
people.
And as with Manhattan and Polaris, together the US and UK are natural allies to work 
together to deliver generational advances. Our two governments should steer, 
facilitate and enable our world-leading industries and scientists to deliver bigger 
outcomes offered by advocacy in technology.
We are at a genuine inflection point. Now is the moment to back the innovators at the 
tech frontier. Instead of over-regulating these new technologies, our two governments 
stand ready to enable and seize the almost unimaginable benefits that they offer. The 
fair exchange of manufactured products is, and will of course remain, important to 
both US and UK economies. But the trade agreement Prime Minster Starmer and 
President Trump left the West Wing seeking a few weeks ago imagines a much 
broader and future-focussed collaboration that looks well beyond goods and services, 
to a full spectrum technology partnership between Britain and America.
I am excited by the breakthroughs such a partnership could accelerate together. In 
Biotech, there is huge potential in genomics, protein design and engineering. Artificial 
Intelligence could enable earlier and more precise diagnosis, better drug discovery 
and personalised cancer vaccines. We are determined to build advanced data centres 
and develop the world’ s first advanced quantum computer. And there are 
opportunities in collaborating to advance nuclear technology. These are just a few 
areas in which the US and UK could create moonshot achievements. Our vision for 
this partnership should span a range of possibilities, on earth and in space.
In an era of heightened competition and contest, we cannot afford to stand still. It is 
vital that our great democracies adapt to counter new threats, retain the edge over our 
adversaries and embrace new opportunities across critical advanced technologies to 
improve the daily lives of all of our citizens. As closely allied democracies, America 
and Britian can achieve more together than they can do apart. This is why a 
technology partnership needs to be at the heart of any US-UK economic deal. We 
need to think MEGA –  Make our Economies Great Again –  for all our prosperity and 
security.
ENDS
Personal
392


Director of Communications & Spokesperson
British Embassy Washington
OFFICIAL
Personal

(Cabinet Office) (Sensitive) Jcs

Subject: Significant progress in agreeing Germany's mega new fiscal package
OFFICIAL

An update on today’s developments on Germany’s defence and infra package. We will diptel
after the Bundesrat vote on Tuesday.
Summary: CDU, SPD and Greens reach agreement on the defence and infrastructure fiscal
package announced by Merz last Tuesday (see Diptel ). This secures the 2/3
Bundestag majority required for the changes. Greens extract concessions from CDU and
SPD that will extend the scale of the stimulus. The package still requires approval by the
Bundesrat.
Detail
1. On Friday, Friedrich Merz announced the outcome of negotiations between the
Greens, SPD and CDU/CSU. Changes to the original proposal were required to secure
Greens approval and confirm that a revolution in German fiscal policy is underway.

2. All three parties affirmed their support for defence spending above 1% to be exempt
from the German debt brake, which limits borrowing to 0.35% of GDP.

397

3. The Greens succeeded, however, in extracting significant amendments to the
proposed €500bn infrastructure fund:
• The extra-budgetary fund of €500bn, previously negotiated between the CDU and
SPD, was originally vaguely earmarked for infrastructure of which €100bn to be
allocated to the German states. Following negotiations with the Greens, the fund will
now also allocate €100bn to climate action (contributing to the goal of reaching climate
neutrality by 2045). The €500bn will be additional to regular spending: it must fund new
investment, not plug gaps in existing budgets.
• The regular budget must now reach an investment quota of 10% before investments
from the special fund are tapped. This Greens proposal ensures additionality by making
the ability to draw down the €500bn dependent on substantial infrastructure spend in the
regular budget.
• The definition of defence has been expanded to include debt for civil protection,
intelligence services, aiding countries who are being attacked in contravention of
international law (Ukraine) and information security. The inclusion of Ukraine spending
is a significant since we understand this to mean that German military, humanitarian and
financial support to Ukraine would now be exempt from the debt brake.
Next steps
4. A vote in the Bundestag is scheduled for Tuesday, followed on Friday 21 March by a
vote in the Bundesrat (where representatives from the German states sit). To pass
through the Bundesrat, votes from Bavaria (and therefore support of the Free Voters
party) will be needed. The Free Voters (a localised party, particularly focused on farming
issues in Bavaria) are currently opposed, but hopes are that they will be brought round
by Bavaria's Christian Social Union.
Reactions
5. Merz has said that they have come up with a package that they can put to a vote
"with a clear conscience", while Lars Klingbeil (Leader of the SPD) noted: "We in the
political centre have laid the foundation for Germany to get back on its feet and to
defend itself".
6. Various economists have been central in advising the government on the fiscal
package. Clemens Fuest, Director of the Munich-based Institute for Economic
Research, welcomed the agreement reached on additionality, arguing that the
infrastructure fund should not result in less money from core budgets being spent on
infrastructure. However, economists also warn of possible risks, including raised
borrowing costs, concerns on effectiveness of spending in defence and infra, as well as
inflationary risks.
 The €500bn infrastructure
investment fund amounts to a stimulus of around 1% of GDP each year for the next ten
years: comparable to the impact on the German economy of the Marshall Plan or
reconstruction of Eastern Germany (1.0% of GDP). Market reaction last week was
strong in relation to both the DAX, Euro and Bund yields. And all of this change should
still keep the German debt/GDP ratio below 85%.
***
398


 | First Secretary, Economic and Financial Policy | British Embassy Berlin
Wilhelmstrasse 70 | Berlin | 10117

Mob: 
Tel:

Personal

From: Kumar lyer
Sent: Saturday, March 22, 2025 6:04:11 PM
To: Peter Mandelson
Subject: Potential visit to DC w/c 9th June.

OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Dear Peter,
I have recently been confirmed as the UK’s next Ambassador to the UN & WTO in 
Geneva (a bit more detail here) and will be visiting Barbara and the team in New York 
w/c 9th June. I wanted to get your advice on whether it could be possible to visit 
Washington at the same time?
As with most things across the world at the moment, the US is key to the changes we 
are about to see across the multi-lateral system and it would be genuinely valuable to 
speak to those aligned to our views (e.g. on the Hill and think tanks) as well as those 
less so before I take up my role in the summer.
I appreciate how busy things must be so completely understand if this isn’t possible 
but if it is then please do let me know who I might be able to work with at the mission.
With thanks,
Kumar

Dear Morgan, dear Olly,
I am glad we are now settled on September for the State Visit. The dates 17-19 September 
work well in a busy ‘back to work’ month in the US (and with UNGA High Level Week 
following on 23/9, and the party conference starting later on 28/9).
I am visiting the WH tomorrow, Monday, to meet with Susie Wiles and I know they are 
anxious to confirm SV dates. Are you content for me firmly to offer the September dates and, 
if acceptable to the President, for us to proceed quickly to the more formal offer? My team 
will also work up with No10/FCDO any follow up points for the Wiles meeting coming out of 
today’s PM-POTUS call.

text:
Innovation and Technol: and others

From: Secretary of State (Peter Kyle) Personal
Sent: Monday, March 31, 2025 16:46
To: Lacey, Benedetta (DSIT) eo ; Bowen, Rhys (DSIT) Personal
Fleming, Joshua (DSIT)
Ferona ; Cannicott, Sam (DSIT) Fersonal ; 22_Shafi, Imran (DSIT)
Personal ; Boardman, Hannah (DSIT) Personal JCS
Personal ; Tammy Sandhu Personal JES
; JCS Jcs
Ademokun, Alexander (DSIT) Personal
Middleton , Emily (DSIT) Personal
Ce: Lyle, Thomas (DSIT) Personal fin, Emran (OSIT) Personal

Subject: Hoover Institution Roundtable
OFFICIAL

DSIT, FCDO Colleagues,

Please find below a readout from DSIT SoS and HMA’s roundtable hosted by the Hoover Institution. To note, as per the
summary, names have been anonymised as the discussion took place under Chatham House Rules.

Thursday 20" March 16:30-18:00, Hoover Institution, Stanford University, 426 Galvez Mall, Palo Alto CA

Attendees: Peter Kyle (SoS, DSIT), _ JCS — Spad, DSIT), Emran Mian (DG, DsiT), JCS ps, psit), Peter
Mandelson (HMA to the US), YCS —(PSto HMA to the US), Tammy Sandhu (CG, BCGSF), JCS

% JCs Brett Carter (Hoover Fellow, Professor, Political Science, University
of Southern California), Erin Carter (Hoover Fellow, Professor, Political Science, University of Southern California),
Dipanjan Deb (Co-Founder and CEO, Francisco Partners), Drew Endy (Professor, Bioengineering, Stanford Senior Fellow,
Hoover Institution), Martin Giles (Assistant Director of Hoover Technology Policy Acceleration, Policy Fellow, Hoover
Institution), Siegfried Glenzer (Professor, Photon Science, Stanford), Andy Grotto (Research Scholar, CIASC, Stanford),
Drew Guevara (Morgan Stanley), Nicholas Harris (Founder and CEO, Lightmatter), Katie Haun (Founder and CEO, Haun
Ventures), Norbert Holtkamp (Professor, Particle Physics and Astrophysics, SLAC, Stanford, Science Fellow, Hoover
Institution), Marc Jones (Chairman and Co-CEO, Aeris Communications), Mykel Kochenderfer (Professor, Astrophysics
and Astronautics, Stanford), Keith Krach (Co-Founder & Chairman, Krach Institute for Tech Diplomacy at Purdue), David
Krane (CEO and Managing Partner, GV), Max Lamparth (Postdoctoral Fellow, Stanford), Herb Lin (Senior Research
Scholar, CISAC, Stanford, Cyber Policy Fellow, Hoover Institution), Enrique Lores (President and CEO, HP Inc.), James
Manyika (Senior Vice President, Google), Ann Mather (Board Director, Netflix), Allison Okamura (Professor, Mechanical
Engineering, Stanford, Science Fellow, Hoover Institution), Ruth Porat (President & Chief Investment Officer, Alphabet
and Google), Anka Reuel (Geopolitics & Technology Fellow, Harvard Kennedy School, Researcher, Trustworthy Al
Research Lab and Stanford Intelligent Systems Lab), Kang Shen (Director, Wu Tsai Neuroscience Institute, Professor,
Biology and Pathology, Stanford), Ram Shriram (Managing Partner, Sherpalo Ventures), Allan

460

Thygesen (CEO, Docusign), Glenn Tiffert (Distinguished Research Fellow, Hoover Institution), Robin Washington (COO
and CFO , Salesforce, Inc.), David Wehner (CSO, Meta), Amy Zegart (Senior Fellow, Hoover Institution, Senior Fellow,
Institute for Human-Centered Al and Freeman-Spogli Institute, Director, Hoover Technology Policy Accelerator).

Summary: this was a wide-ranging discussion about science and technology policy held under Chatham House rules
(hence specific contributions from participants have been anonymised), which focused on (i) support for basic
research, (ii) regulation (where attendees shared varied views about the appropriate balance between regulation and
innovation), (iii) UK-US relations and (iv) technology adoption.

[begins]

The roundtable started with introductions from two attendees. One summarised relevant findings of the 2025
Stanford Emerging Technologies Review, noting the importance of partnership in a world where many crucial decision-
makers are now outside of government. The other said that the UK’s Al Action Plan was a model for the world both in
the engagement that went into developing it and in the ambition and specificity of its recommendations.

Peter Kyle (PK) then gave short introductory remarks. He noted that, having listened, it was now time for HMG to
deliver, focusing on (1) economic growth, (2) the productivity and efficiency of government and (3) transforming
citizens’ experience of the state. He outlined recent work on infrastructure, touching on the Action Plan (noting the
number of responses to the recent call for Eols), CNI designation for data centres and planning reforms. He spoke
about regulation and the work of the RIO, the uplift to R&D in a challenging fiscal context and the shift in HMG’s
approach towards a focus on high-growth sectors.

Peter Mandelson (PM) began by emphasising that he has a clear remit — to update the UK-US relationship and put
tech partnership at its heart. This will ensure that, in the new global context, we can win a new and profound
technological race against our rivals and adversaries, PM referenced the work of the Manhattan Project, which
unlocked new discoveries, technologies and industries. He also spoke about a new model of alignment which would
require alignment not just government to government, but also between universities and the private sector — in PM's
view, entrepreneurs should be ‘engines of collaboration. PM said that this technological partnership would be a
natural extension of our unique defence and security partnership.

The ensuing discussion encompassed the full range of science and tech policy, which | have grouped below broadly by
theme:

Research:
* The importance of investing in basic research and supporting commercial spinout.

* Sustainable funding for research, One attendee noted how the tuition fee cap in the UK and potential taxes on
university endowments in the US threatened funding, and also touched on the need to reform immigration,
systems to ensure that the most talented people can stay in the UK. PK spoke about (i) the university finance
situation HMG had inherited and the measures he had taken to resolve it (including increases in the HE and
research budgets, entailing higher expectations from universities) (ii) ending the “war on universities” and the
importance of predictability and stability as a starting point, (iii) the industrial strategy as an example of this
approach.

* The importance of ensuring that rigorous research security measures do not end up accidentally making
collaboration between friends (e.g. the UK and the US) more difficult or imposing excessive bureaucracy. HMA
agreed with this point, emphasising the need to (i) throw a wall around our collective work and (ii) increase
collaboration within that wall, taking advantage of our exceptional levels of mutual trust and confidence.

Regulation:

* Alregulation. PK, when asked by one attendee how our approach differs to the EU, emphasised the
fundamental difference in approach, stressing (i) we have not legislated in the UK (though he has not ruled this
out), (ii) the AISI has real value as a partner for frontier labs to support the safe deployment of new models, (iii)
the maturity of our regulatory landscape and work to empower sector-specific regulators and (iv) the
importance of innovation for national security. Another attendee later spoke about the EU’s approach and
noted the difficulty of evaluating approaches to regulation well, stressing the need for technical expertise and
the UK’s leadership in this space. PK later spoke about his decision to rebrand the AISI as a ‘Security Institute’
and explained its work on chemical and biological threats, emphasising the importance of openness for safety.
He also spoke about the value of a voluntary of approach and the importance of constructive relationships
between governments and labs.

° Al Copyright.
* Regulatory alignment. One attendee noted that, whilst the UK’s overall regulatory approach had generally

managed to strike the right balance, we must be wary of the trade-offs between (a) finding optimal regulation
for the UK and (b) ensuring consistency with other countries, noting the example of digital IDs, where

461


alignment is important. He noted that the UK can set a positive example for the EU on data transfers and data 
localisation. Another attendee noted the example of data-sharing in aviation, where there are clear incentives 
to share data, including about mistakes, to improve collective safety.
• Bitcoin regulation. One attendee noted the need to reform regulation for stablecoins in particular. PK noted
that he was excited by the potential of bitcoin, including the opportunities that blockchain technologies present
for decentralisation. He noted that no country had solved this issue, but that we were working on it.
US-UK Relations: 
• HMG's Rresence in SF. One attendee noted that the UK's team in SF should be better known, given the need for
a constant conversation between the UK and the US 'on the round.'
• The UK-US tech RartnershiR.,_One attendee_asked PK what his expectations were from the US tech sector. PK
emphasised that he was looking for a partnership which would ensure that our two countries could get ahead
in the technological race and stay there, noting that this is crucial for our prosperity and our national security.
He noted that the offer we are currently proposing to the USG is only possible because of the depth of our
history, which we must now leverage to take on our shared challenges. PK noted the UK's strengths, including
four of the top ten universities and a mature investment landscape, noting the need to coordinate this
landscape and make it more concerted.
AdoRtion, Infrastructure and Growth: 
• Technologv. adoRtion. One attendee noted that the UK has a strong science and R&D base but noted that
translating this into productivity gains would require a focus on (i) adoption, (ii) energy infrastructure and (iii)
specific use-cases like healthcare. He noted that this approach should be applied to sectors like life sciences and
quantum, not just Al. PK noted the need to be creative about adoption and explained the work of the Tech
Adoption Review to get our interventions right and deliver tangible change. PK also noted the work of the Al
Energy Council.
Misc.: 
• The importance of advanced semiconductor manufacturing_for sovereignty. One attendee noted that we had
fallen behind rival and adversary states and concluded that the UK must invest.
• The value and potential of low-tech innovation.
Conclusion: 
• The roundtable ended with a quickfire round where attendees were asked to share their views about the one
technology that would define the decade to come - answers varied a great deal, varying from cybersecurity
and quantum technologies to education and inclusion.
 I Speechwriter to the Secretary of State I Department for Science, Innovation and Technology I 
OFFICIAL 
JCS
JCS
462

492

To: BEW Monitoring PERSONAL
Subject: MONITORING: Scott Bessent, Tucker Carlson Interview Transcript: Tariffs, Trade, Economic Policy -
4/4/2025

OFFICIAL
Some people who received this message don't often get email from PERSONAL . Learn why this is important
OFFICIAL

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent sat down with Tucker Carlson for an hour-long interview to explain
the administration’ s new tariffs, and why they had to do something to stop the slide. “And if we think
like a classical model of tariff income, would say, if there's a 10% tariff, then the currency would
appreciate about 40% of that. So 4% of it, then the producer in the other country would eat about 4%
and then the US consumer might have a one time price adjustment of 2% so in a 10% tariff, maybe
the consumer pays 2% of it. We saw there was a study out recently from a group at MIT that shows
with President Trump's first China tariffs, which were approximately 20% the price level went up point
seven. So to answer your question, if we could put on a 20% tariff and have the foreigners pay that
and use that money to bring down our government deficit and keep taxes low.” He also talked about
his recent interaction with Ukraine President Selensky:

Tucker Carlson
How did you wind up interacting with Zelensky?
Scott Bessent

President asked me to go to, well, he actually asked me to lead the economic agreement that was
part of his peace plan. And | think it's important to look at the arc of President Trump's peace plan,
and he had sequenced it really well, was we're going to sign a deal with Ukrainians, and it'll do several
things. One, it will make the US and Ukraine partners to link us closer together. Two, it willbe a
symbol to Russian leadership that the US isn't abandoning Ukraine. But importantly, three, it will
show the American people that we have an economic stake and that we haven't just been doing these
massive grants, which has been the history of USAID, of course. So Ukraine succeeds, we succeed,
and it could be a long term partnership. Now, President, | thought it was important to take the

gotten in, I worry a lot, a little less, but I still worry we've got a tremendous amount of debt to roll, and 
I worry that we aren't going to do the smart cuts and the focus on waste, fraud and abuse. I worry that 
that gets sidelined. And then on the other side, I worry that the tax bill somehow gets bogged down. 
501


We can have the largest tax increase in history. Or I worry that the normal geopolitical things, whether 
it's Iran Taiwan, that something goes haywire between Russia and Ukraine. 
Tucker Carlson   
So you began your job description by saying you're a bond salesman. You're selling America's debt to 
the world. And I know that was like a big part of the portfolio as the transition was envisioning it like, 
who can make the case for American bonds to the world? How were your case? Are you confident you 
can do that? And how is your pitch changing after yesterday? 
Scott Bessent   
Well, as I said, right right now, we're in this strange betwixt and between, because we are going to 
take in substantial tariff revenue, and what Doge is doing is substantially cutting expenses, but we're 
not getting credit for it right now, but I think markets starting to get a hint of it. So the 10 year bond, 
having almost peaked at five, is now through four. Way to think about it is every 100 every basis 
points, about a billion dollars in savings. So we've saved $100 billion and that's probably not going to 
happen that quickly again, but I think that we are setting the sales for much better fiscal time, and 
people don't have to say the US is out of control, the US is going to default that we're not. And I think 
every day my case gets better, and like if we were to just imagine a very reductionist formula for 
government, so g equals s minus t, spending minus taxes, right that for spending. Our side, the 
Republicans, likes to we like to spend, but less than the Democrats. But both sides like to spend and 
then minus t. Democrats like higher taxes. We like lower taxes. What if the s actually went down? And 
I think, to me, that's the exciting part that that's been unthinkable in everybody's calculation.  
Tucker Carlson   
No one's even really seriously raised it, including Ronald Reagan, by the way. 
Scott Bessent   
Yep. And look, I think President Reagan had a different agenda, that he ramped up defense spending. 
And remember, Oh, he's crazy. Is this? It's going to break the budget? Star Wars is crazy. He's a mad 
man. And then the iron curtain went down. Yes, we were able to so it's actually a Soviet term, and he 
used the Soviet strategy on this, on the cell, on the Russians escalate to de escalate. Yes. So he 
escalated military spending. They tried to keep up, and then they collapsed. So, you know, again, 
everything's easy in hindsight, but I think here what we're doing in terms of bringing down these 
incredible levels of spending, but more More importantly, I tell anyone who will listen, I said, 
Remember, Doge is the office of government efficiency, not elimination, Not extinction. So what if we 
can actually do a better job with what with less seems inconceivable, but when I when I see what's 
going on in a lot of these government agencies, it's unbelievable. And like, if you think I've lived in 
Manhattan, I've lived in Florida, roughly, state of New York, state of Florida, have the same number of 
people. Actually, Florida has few more now. So New York budget is about 235 billion. Florida budgets, 
120 5 billion. How did they do that? Oh yeah. And there's no income tax in Florida. Oh yeah, and the 
roads are better, and you go and get your driver's license, and it takes 15 minutes, not five hours. So I 
think, can we make the rest of America look more like Florida and less like New York? Or one day, is 
New York gonna look more like Florida? 
Tucker Carlson   
So do you think after four years, we will see an actual net reduction in government spending tha nks to 
Doge? 
Scott Bessent   
Inflation adjusted? Yes. 
502


Tucker Carlson   
How long do you think Elon is in for? 
Scott Bessent   
I think that Elon that day to day, I don't know, but I think his imprint is going to be with us a long time, 
and I think that the mainstream media has tried to demonize Doge and all the folks who work with 
him. And I think we have a couple of treasury a couple of IRS. They're Treasury Employees who I hired, 
I interviewed, and I'll tell you, there's this one fellow, Tom Kraus. He was on TV with Brett bear the 
other night, and Tom's incredible. He's done $100 billion of tech mergers with my investment 
business. I would have hired him. I don't think I could have afforded him, and he is doing this for the 
good of his country. He came in without touching any of the pavement systems that the mistruths 
that were spread by the Washington Post. He has analyzed the system, and within six weeks, pointed 
at all the vulnerabilities there. There's a young man named Sam kirkos Who was on Laura Ingraham 
with me the other night, and that was my idea. It's like, Let's bring him out of the shadows, and let's 
make this demonization stopped. And like this young man, other than fact, he only owns one pair of 
pants, which he likes, telling everyone, you or I would be happy if our daughter brought him home. 
He's a patriot. He's working hard for the country, and he's analytical, and the things that he's seen at 
the IRS just in their tech systems, and that's all he does. You know, Elon has a shirt that he wears 
under his jacket. He'll flash every once in a while, it says tech support, and that's what we're getting. 
Is we've got a blockbuster style government, and we should be on Netflix. We're hailing cabs and 
we're in an Uber economy. Why can't the government be in the gig economy? Not hard. So we're not 
positive 
Tucker Carlson   
Can I see a weird question, what? Who runs the Federal Reserve? Exactly. I don't really. How can you 
have this, like, Pivotal entity, institution that obviously has, like, the most direct effect on markets, 
many and it does seem outside of political control. 
Scott Bessent   
What is that? Well, look, I think that I during my confirmation hearing, and I was actually at a dinner 
that Jerome Powell was at last night at my confirmation hearing, I said I'm only going to talk about the 
mistakes the Federal Reserve has made in the past. I won't talk about the ones they're going to make 
in the future. But I think it is important for monetary policy that they are walled off, that some of the 
other things they do, what regulation. They got into Dei, they got into climate, I actually think that that 
impinges on their monetary policy and makes them vulnerable. So I think that they should focus on 
monetary policy, doing the best that they can for the American economy, the American people, 
keeping inflation low. And then the rest. There are a lot of other banking agencies too.  
Tucker Carlson   
So the Fed Controls the Weather now too. What were they doing with climate? 
Scott Bessent   
Well, I will tell you, there's something that the Treasury Secretary chairs, something called the 
Financial Stability Oversight Council, and it's all the financial regulators. And I think it was two weeks 
before Silicon Valley Bank went under. F socks issued a report, and guess what? They said the biggest 
risk to the financial system was climate, really not that there was a large bank in California that was 
having a slow motion run on its assets, and that the it would cause another bank failure than another 
bank failure was climate. So as far as I can see, climate's been pretty good. They've they failed. The 
regulators failed. And I think that's what people are getting sick of. And it's back to my one of my 
favorite phrases that President Trump uses common sense, yes, like it's just common sense that they 
if you have a whole bunch of deposit that could leave your bank with a click, you shouldn't have all 
503


these long term assets, but they were too busy worrying about the weather. And there was also some 
degree of regulatory capture. Seems hard to believe to the average citizen, the CEO of Silicon Valley 
Bank was on the board of the San Francisco Federal Reserve, who was his chief regulator? They're 
going to tell that guy what to do. 
Tucker Carlson   
Why is gold moving around the world in such huge quantities right now? 
Scott Bessent   
Wella couple of things. It's moving physically. It's moving because of potential tariffs here. So, it was 
unclear whether we were going to exempt gold from tariffs, which I believe we have. So there was a 
big move out of alts in Switzerland, out of alts in London, to get it into New York and look that there 
are a lot of different stores of value. Over time, Bitcoin is becoming a store of value. Gold has 
historically been a store of value. I think what's interesting is, where do we see the gold demand 
coming from? Huge amount is from China. Yes, where, as I said, they're in the middle of an economic 
recession, slash depression. People don't trust the Chinese currency because they have capital 
controls. There are 1.1 point 4 billion Chinese who won't want to get their money out, and they won't 
let them. They will let them buy gold. 
Tucker Carlson   
So that's the response you think. But it's just, do you find it interesting that even now 2025 when 
everything is abstract and digital, that gold is still widely believed globally to be a reliable store of 
value.  
Scott Bessent   
I think that store gold, gold has a lot of history going with it. That friend of mine's grandmother during 
Russia had a financial crisis in 98 then they had a big inflation, and a friend of mine's grandmother 
went out and bought 18 bicycles and put them in her apartment. And that was her store of value, yes. 
Tucker Carlson   
So how did she do on the bike investment? Great, great. I wish I did that well. 
Scott Bessent   
But that look gold, gold also has other applications, a lot of applications jewelry in India, but it's 
something that historically, people have all agreed on, and gold does not have gold can't have a fiscal 
problem. Gold cannot have a gigantic budget deficit. Gold cannot have the a a war. So just the fact 
that it is this isolated thing makes it very interesting, and the fact that the entire global trading system 
until Richard Nixon took us off, was tied to gold. So you're not anti gold. Oh no, no, no, I they when I 
had my fun. I think people might have called me a gold bug. 
Tucker Carlson   
How did you wind up interacting with Zelensky? 
Scott Bessent   
President asked me to go to, well, he actually asked me to lead the economic agreement that was 
part of his peace plan. And I think it's important to look at the arc of President Trump's peace plan, 
and he had sequenced it really well, was we're going to s ign a deal with Ukrainians, and it'll do several 
things. One, it will make the US and Ukraine partners to link us closer together. Two, it will be a 
symbol to Russian leadership that the US isn't abandoning Ukraine. But importantly, three, it will 
show the American people that we have an economic stake and that we haven't just been doing these 
massive grants, which has been the history of USAID, of course. So Ukraine succeeds, we succeed, 
504


and it could be a long term partnership. Now, President, I thought it was important to take the 
agreement to Kyiv and present it to President Zelensky. Everyone's telling me, oh no, have him meet 
you in Vienna. He's going to be at the Munich Security Conference. A few days later, I said, No, I want 
to go there and discuss it with him. And that's how I ended up going to Kyiv. Very interesting. Going to 
Kyiv flight of Poland. Then you get on, I don't know if you've been on it, get on this night train for 10 
hours, and you arrive at Kyiv at 830 in the morning. And for anyone who they said, Oh, the President 
Trump selling out to the Russians. Not selling out to the Russians. The Russians bombed Kyiv four 
hours before I got there. They hadn't bombed since November. So the Russians didn't like the look of 
this deal, because it thought that the it was actually something durable for the US people and the 
Ukrainian people. So what happened? President Zelensky was in the mood to sign the deal that day. 
We had a spirited discussion, and I said to him, you've got 50 reporters out there. I am here to show 
that there is no daylight between the American people the Russian people, between, excuse me, 
Ukrainian people, between the American leadership and Ukrainian leadership. And I said, you've 
opened up daylight size of the Grand Canyon. What are we going to go out and tell those people? He 
said, Tell them I'm going to sign the deal in Munich. I said, so you're going to sign the deal when you 
see vice president Vance and Secretary of State Rubio in Munich? Yes, he didn't sign it there. There's 
a lot of back and forth. The following week, they're begging to come to the White House, and should 
make him sign the deal before he comes. And then he got to the Oval Office and blew up. What 
should have been the easiest thing to do in the world. He's supposed to show up, have a press 
conference. We're going to have a private lunch. If he had anything on his mind, there were going to 
be nine of us, nine of them in the White House dining room he could have aired his grievances in. And 
there's a famous photo in the East Wing ballroom of everything lay down on the table to be signed, 
and it never got signed.         
Tucker Carlson   
So this is an unelected president of a client state whose bureaucrats are being paid directly by 
American taxpayers, including their retirement accounts are funded by us. 
Scott Bessent   
and they have the lowest retirement age in Europe. It's 60 put it in contrast, France is 62 and Italy 67 
Tucker Carlson   
and we're paying for that. And so at a certain point, you know, you wouldn't expect a man like that in a 
highly precarious position. I think it's fair to say to, like, assume a high handed tone with American 
officials and berate them and sniff a lot and say, you know, it's basically act like a crazy person, like, 
Well, how do you account for that, the arrogance, what is that 
Scott Bessent   
look a couple of things. Is he was a performer, yes, kind of a vaudevillian, and he was an ordinary 
person thrown into a fraught time, rose to the occasion. Yes. Was heroic, yes. And I think is stuck, 
and I think under a tremendous amount of pressure. I think that some of the government officials 
around him and the cabinet are very good. I think some of the people around him, he's not getting the 
best advice that his advisors are not perfect. And I think if we go back to the agreement, I think one of 
the great things about the agreement is it makes sure that the money comes to the American people 
and the Ukrainian people. Because Tucker, let me tell you what the agreement was. It wasn't one of 
these rapacious Chinese deals where it's, sign over your mind, sign over your report, or it wasn't a 
loan to own. Here's a loan. You're never going to be able to pay it back. We're going to get this. It's a 
genuine economic partnership that they put in assets. We can put in loans from our overseas banks. 
We put in American know how, and we didn't get it. We don't make any money unless they make 
money. And, you know, who doesn't like that? People with their hand in the till, right, right? Because 
we were regulating the flow of the money. So the I think that's part of the glitch. I am hopeful that the 
505


that it was a bug, not a feature of the system, and that that's been fixed. We're expecting a Ukraine 
Ukrainian technical team beginning of next week, and I'm hopeful we can get this thing signed and go 
back to a win, win situation, because the President Trump, I didn't give you the full arc of the deal. 
President Trump wanted to create the this deal with the Ukrainians, then be able to go to Russian 
leadership with and show them that we do stand with the Ukrainians, but they on an economic basis, 
and then the Russians would be incentivized to come to the table also, and so that the sequences 
sequencing has been thrown off, but I think we can fix it.  
Tucker Carlson 
Are we ever going to get an audit of where all of our billions went, where all the weapons we sent, 
what happened, all the cash, all the pallets of $100 bills, like, where are they now? Why? Why is it 
hard to get an audit of what happened in the money? 
Scott Bessent   
I don't know Tucker in my life, I always try to look forward, and I think if this deal works, then that 
money could end up being small change. Yes, a good way to think about it. When the Iron Curtain 
came down, Ukraine and Poland's economies were roughly the same size. Poland is three and a half 
or four times larger now, yes, Poland is going to have one of the highest per capita GDPs in the world, 
higher than some Western European countries, for sure, Ukraine, to your point, because of various 
kleptocracy aspects that have been held back. Yes, so hopefully, with US assistance and President 
Trump engineering this peace deal, Ukrainian people can have a better future for a treasury secretary. 
Tucker Carlson   
You're a very capable diplomat, I must say, very diplomatic language. Last question, how do you so? 
Because this is also news, going back to the tariff announcement yesterday, and because it's like it 
really is a total departure from what all of us have grown up with and our expectations and however 
much you support it, it's like it's new, and people are freaked out about it. How do you keep message 
discipline in an administration, in any administration, and this one, specifically, how do you decide 
how you're going to explain this, who's going to explain it? How do you keep messages from 
contradicting one another. What does that process like?  
Scott Bessent   
We will take President Trump's lead. Yeah, we know we had a meeting yesterday morning. We had a 
meeting after the announcement in the Rose Garden before we went out doing media. And as you 
know, he's his own best spokesman. Yes, nobody can do it better. And then we fan out and we are all 
unified behind this and his vision. That's why we're here. If you didn't want to be part of it, shouldn't be 
here. 
Tucker Carlson   
But you ever say like, you know, maybe this is someone who can reassure potential bond buyers, 
investors in markets, this person scares the crap out of markets, like, let's get the guy who reassures 
markets. 
Scott Bessent   
Well, the one thing I will say that no one should listen to anyone in the markets talk about the US 
dollar, other than President Trump or myself. We are the only ones who speak for this administration, 
the United States Government on dollar policy. I didn't tell you we have a strong dollar policy, and 
we're putting in all of the necessary ingredients to make sure the dollar is strong over the long run. 
Can it go up and down on a Bloomberg terminal over the short run? Sure? Can individual bilateral 
moves happen between the dollar and the Mexican currency, the dollar in the yen. Sure, that's that's 
what I made my living going, I'm glad it doesn't. But over time, if we put in solid economic 
506


fundamentals the and do this transformative program, then the dollar is going to do great, and the 
American people are going to debris. 
Tucker Carlson   
Secretary, thank you very much. Thank you. 
Scott Bessent 
Appreciate it.

From: JCS

To: BEW Monitoring

Subject: MONITORING: Stephen Miller, Fox News Interview Transcript: Tariffs, Stock
Markets, Global Trade Imbalances, Europe - 4/4/2025


OFFICIAL
White House Deputy Chief of Staff, Stephen Miller, did a Fox News 
broadcast interview with Sandra Smith. During the conversation Miller 
defended President Trump’ s new tariff’ s and the need for them “ The 
United States is 36% of global consumption, and we're more than 25% of 
the world's economy, just the United States alone. That means we get to 
set the rules and terms for international trade”  He also referenced 
Europe several times:
Stephen Miller
Our leaders allowed foreign countries to rig the rules of the game, to
cheat, to steal, to rob, to plunder, that has cost America trillions of dollars
in wealth. These countries cannot win a trade fight with us, because
they're already the ones who have cheated. So in other words, they've
stolen our industries. We have all the upside here in this fight, because we
can now make these industries come back, because we have the market.
We have the consumers they need. Japan cannot replace the American
automobile market. Europe can't replace the American automobile 
market.
They need us. And this is a fundamental issue of fairness and national
security.”
“We protect and defend all of Europe, and it costs them trillions of dollars
over time. And what do they do? They run an over $300 million trade
deficit with the United States. Why should America protect the world, send
foreign aid to the world, defend the world, provide for the world, in
exchange get ripped off by every other country in the world.”
Please find a rough transcript below:
Sandra Smith
All right. Fox news alert here, as the selling does seem to continue in the 
American stock market, you've got the Dow sinking to the lows of the 
session as we speak right now, down 2100, points at 38,418. Okay, so 
let's bring in Stephen Miller. He's at the White House. He's the White 
House Deputy Chief of Staff. Your timing is perfect, because I'm sure 
there are a lot of folks out there who would like to know the White House 
thinking here, Stephen, please do share with us your thoughts in this 
moment, and especially after China announced these retaliatory tariffs, it 
seems like that's when the markets took another leg down here. Where is 
all of this going next?
Stephen Miller
Well, let's first establish an important foundation. The United States is 36% 
of global consumption, and we're more than 25% of the world's economy, 
just the United States alone. That means we get to set the rules and terms 
for international trade. But we've done the opposite as a country for 
decades now, when we hear the term globalization, what does that really 
mean? It means taking America's wealth and shipping it to foreign 
510


countries. So all the factories that used to be in America are now in China, 
Cambodia, Thailand, Vietnam, Canada, Mexico, parts of Africa, India, 
Europe, Japan, Korea, you know where they’ re not? the United States of 
America. Because our leaders allowed foreign countries to rig the rules of 
the game, to cheat, to steal, to rob, to plunder, that has cost America 
trillions of dollars in wealth. These countries cannot win a trade fight with 
us, because they're already the ones who have cheated. So in other 
words, they've stolen our industries. We have all the upside here in this 
fight, because we can now make these industries come back, because we 
have the market. We have the consumers they need. Japan cannot 
replace the American automobile market. Europe can't replace the 
American automobile market. They need us. And this is a fundamental 
issue of fairness and national security. Tell me. Why is it that if you go to 
Tokyo, there's no American cars if you go to Berlin, there's no American 
cars, but on our streets we have every foreign car there is in the world. 
They've locked their markets to our cars, they have stolen from our 
workers and our people, and President Trump is saying that has to end. 
We need our wealth back and our jobs back and the quality of America, 
the American quality of life will rise exponentially as these policies kick in, 
and as the great onshoring and return of American wealth occurs. 
Sandra Smith
Are you confident we can replace that production, and we can do so in a 
way that Americans can actually afford, and like those cars?
Stephen Miller
without question. In fact, the changes in advanced manufacturing 
technology, 3d printing, robotics, artificial intelligence. This makes it more 
and more affordable to manufacture, produce and build at scale in the 
United States. American workers should not have to compete with child 
slave wages in Vietnam. They should not have to. That is wrong. We do 
not need a global race to the bottom that says that American workers have 
to earn the same wages and work in the same conditions as the poorest 
workers in the third world. That's the system that we've had with the right 
tax policy, the right regulation policy, the right trade policy, and investing in 
advanced manufacturing, all of these industries and supply chains can 
come back home. But here's the thing, it's not a choice. It's a national 
security imperative. What would happen if there was a war and we didn't 
have the ability to manufacture our own steel, our own aluminum, our own 
medicine, our own automobiles, our own aircraft? What would happen to 
the United States of America? It is a fundamental matter of national 
survival.
Sandra Smith
Yeah, well, we got a glimpse of that during COVID right when people 
couldn't get what they needed. We learned so much of it was being made 
outside of the United States. And how about the food industry? This was 
really interesting perspective right here on America Reports last hour from 
a shrimper. Listen.
Craig Reave (Sea Eagle Seafood Owner)
511


we've been getting killed by imports for not just years, but literally 
decades. So we've been suffering for a long, long time, and these tariffs, 
we believe are going to give us some immediate relief. We've outsourced 
our whole industry. 94% of the shrimp consumed in the United States is 
imported.
Sandra Smith
Okay so he shared with us, he's optimistic that this could help his industry 
survive, and they've seen a decline for decades now, because all of these, 
you know, all the shrimps been coming in from all these other countries 
into the United States, low quality shrimp, he pointed out, but there's. 
Stephen Miller
By the way, a lot of that, a lot of the seafood that we're importing has 
formaldehyde in it, is not safe for consumption, and this is this whole 
situation. People have to understand just how badly we've been ripped off 
as a country. You heard that gentleman, 94% of ship is imported to the 
United States, and we have the Gulf of America. The US agriculture and 
seafood industry has been decimated because we allow for subpar, cheap 
foreign imports without limit that are subsidized by foreign countries to put 
our farmers and our shrimpers out of business. Well, their markets are 
close to ours. Can someone explain to me, we protect and defend South 
Korea, and in exchange, they steal our automobile industry, they steal our 
electronics industry. We protect and defend Japan, and exchange they 
close their market to our cars. We protect and defend Canada, and they 
run up a massive trade deficit with us. We protect and defend all of 
Europe, and it costs them trillions of dollars over time. And what do they 
do? They run an over $300 million trade deficit with the United States. 
Why should America protect the world, send foreign aid to the world, 
defend the world, provide for the world, in exchange get ripped off by 
every other country in the world. What we have to show for it, the 
deindustrialization of the heartland, the destruction of the American 
Dream, the eradication of our industrial and manufacturing base that we 
need for national security and the crushing of the American dream. It has 
to stop, not to mention $36 trillion in debt.
Sandra Smith
I’ ve only got a few seconds left, but people are throwing around the R 
word, and they see the Dow down 2100 points. And people are worried 
about a recession. They're worried that we can't do this without sending 
this economy into such a deep dive that we can't come back from it. And I 
know you guys are all about growth. I know you're about lowering taxes, 
rolling back regulations, and seeing that growth. Are you concerned that 
this is going to put us into a recession?
Stephen Miller
The exact opposite. We inherited from Joe Biden, an economy in total free 
fall. There has been zero jobs growth for American workers in four years. 
In other words, all jobs growth had gone to foreign workers. That is a 
depression, not a recession. That is a depression for the American worker. 
512


We had debt and deficits that were rising unsustainably that would lead us 
into a certain debt and deficit crisis. We had inflation that was in high 
double digits, crushing the American family, making it impossible to afford 
the essentials of life. We had an economy that was in a state of calamity 
and catastrophe. President Trump's policy of reshoring our supply chains, 
pushing for total expensing of capital gains, eliminating regulation, 
eliminating intrusive green, new scam environmental rules, pushing for the 
biggest, broadest tax cut in American history, and reducing taxes on 
American workers, including ending taxes on tips, ending taxes on 
overtime, ending taxes on Social Security. This economic program is the 
single most important program for American wealth, prosperity and 
rejuvenation that we've ever seen in this country.
Sandra Smith
I’d love to continue the conversation hope to have you back on soon 
Steven, thank you so much.
Stephen Miller
Thank you

description: 185 - 15-04-2025 - Meeting between Peter Mandelson and Graeme Biggar (Director General of the National Crime Agency)
text:
General of the National Crime Agency)

Subject: [OS] Readout - DG NCA and HMA Washington intro 15.04.25
23 Apr 2025, 08:13

Office of the DG
Personal

to BIGGAR, Graeme, Robert Jones - DG Operations, JCS

, DGs Private Offices Only, NCAS
Engagement, Director Intel Private Office, Director Strategy Private Office

OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

Dear Graeme,

Jcs

With many thanks to ““*, please see below a readout of your H/S introductory call

with HMA Lord Peter Mandelson on 15" April.
Attendees

Graeme Biggar - DG NCA
Lord Peter Mandelson - HMA Washington
JCS = —Regional Manager, North America
JCS — - Head of Operations North America and The Caribbean

JCS = —Senior Manager, North America and Caribbean
JCS - DGPO
Readout

Following brief introductions, HMA asked about how the National Crime Agency fits
into the wider LE/Policing and whether there was tension between the NCA and local

Police forces. You gave a full explanation about the UK LE landscape and how the

534

NCA fits in, being the national lead on SOC and setting the strategic direction across 
Policing; you also covered his concern about possible tensions. 
Data Access / online activity / combating the use of data to facilitate crime was on 
HMA’s mind.  You explained the change we have seen in criminality and how it has 
moved ‘online’, particularly fraud, cybercrime and CSAE, but you also pointed out 
how almost all other crime has a data angle, due to phone/device usage. 
You spoke in detail about the UKUSDAA 
. At HMA’s request, you explained the 
DAA and E2EE to him in detail,  highlighting the difference between lawful access 
and content moderation, but how both were impacted by E2EE.  You informed HMA 
about our ILO in NCMEC and how NCMEC operates - millions of reports from tech 
companies being triaged via NCMEC, resulting in global referrals. You informed HMA 
that these referrals lead to 800 arrests per month and 1000 children being 
safeguarded as a result. HMA was visibly shocked by the figures you quoted. You 
went on to explain that we estimate that between 1.3 and 1.6% of adults in the UK 
(approximately 800,000) pose a serious sexual risk to children and how tech 
companies signpost us to this material, for us to act upon, hopefully preventing 
contact abuse. 
AP 1: You suggested HMA visited NCMEC, which he was extremely keen to do, and 
I agreed to arrange (16/4 update: visit confirmed for 13/5).  
HMA enquired about your ‘general’ counterparts, and you spoke about the FBI, DEA, 
HSI & USSS. You talked about the opportunities for collaboration even with the 
change in priority to drugs/OIC on the SW border. You spoke about cyber and online 
crimes, detailing our tremendous success with the use of US sanctions tools to 
tackle Russian ransomware attacks. 
 
HMA was due to meet with META that afternoon and sought your views.  You 
discussed how valuable they were particularly in relation to fraud and that META was 
the biggest contributor to NCMEC cyber tips, but you expressed concern about the 
impact of E2EE. 
 
***
***
***
535


HMA asked for any final information he should be aware of, and you briefly explained 
our footprint in the USA and how our Miami office also covers the Caribbean – 
 
Overall, a very positive first meeting, with HMA’s interest and engagement increasing 
as he learned more about the NCA and our mission, setting the tone for additional 
engagement at post. 
Thanks, 
 
 
Staff Officer to Director General 
National Crime Agency 
 
JCS
Personal
***
536
